topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a real-time evolutionary generative framework that integrates incomplete mutation data with rapid viral protein structure prediction to enable fast, adaptive modeling of viral protein variants beyond static AlphaFold predictions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current protein structure prediction methods, including AlphaFold2, do not incorporate real-time evolutionary dynamics or effectively handle incomplete and rapidly evolving viral mutation data, limiting their use in epidemiological surveillance during outbreaks. This gap prevents timely structural characterization of emerging viral variants, delaying insights critical for public health interventions.

APPROACH:  
We propose EvoViralFold, a hybrid framework combining a probabilistic generative evolutionary model with a fast, structure-aware neural network that predicts plausible viral protein variants under incomplete mutation information. EvoViralFold models mutation trajectories using a stochastic evolutionary simulator conditioned on real-time genomic surveillance data, then rapidly refines predicted variant sequences into 3D structures by integrating a lightweight graph neural network trained on AlphaFold-generated embeddings and known viral protein conformations. This approach enables continuous updating and prioritization of structurally relevant variants amid incomplete data streams.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate EvoViralFold on the following datasets: GISAID SARS-CoV-2 spike protein sequences with temporal mutation labels, CATH v4.3 viral protein domain structures, and the Influenza Research Database (IRD) hemagglutinin variants. Baselines include AlphaFold2, RoseTTAFold, and DeepFragLib. Metrics measured are: (1) top-5 structural RMSD improvement over baseline predictions for emerging variants (target >2 Å absolute improvement), (2) runtime latency reduction by ≥50% compared to AlphaFold2 on mutated sequences, and (3) variant prioritization accuracy via precision-recall against experimentally confirmed escape mutants (target >10% absolute improvement in average precision). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs within a 1-week compute budget, with 5 random seeds and ablation studies isolating effects of evolutionary simulation and structure refinement components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike AlphaFold2 and DeepFragLib, EvoViralFold explicitly incorporates real-time evolutionary dynamics and incomplete mutation information into a fast, end-to-end variant prediction pipeline, enabling rapid, structurally plausible modeling of viral protein variants that static structure predictors cannot provide, thus bridging a critical gap for epidemiological surveillance and response.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a unified framework combining aggressive model pruning, 8-bit quantization, and knowledge distillation with dynamic neural architecture search (NAS) to enable ultra-low-latency, high-accuracy protein structure prediction directly on edge devices, achieving real-time inference (<50 ms) with minimal accuracy degradation (<1% TM-score loss).

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art protein folding models like AlphaFold2 achieve remarkable accuracy but incur high computational and memory costs, resulting in inference latencies that exceed practical limits for edge devices, preventing real-time, on-site applications such as rapid diagnostics or portable biosensing. Existing compression approaches, such as latency-optimized pruning and quantization, trade off accuracy for speed but do not fully exploit model architecture adaptation or knowledge transfer to maintain accuracy under extreme compression and low-precision constraints. Thus, the open problem is enabling real-time (<50 ms) protein folding predictions on resource-constrained edge hardware while preserving AlphaFold-level accuracy.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-stage pipeline: (1) apply structured pruning combined with 8-bit quantization to reduce model size and operations; (2) use knowledge distillation from the full-precision AlphaFold2 model to a lightweight student network to recover predictive accuracy; (3) integrate a dynamic NAS algorithm tailored for edge constraints to automatically identify optimal subnetworks balancing latency and accuracy; (4) implement hardware-aware optimization targeting specific edge TPU and mobile GPU architectures. The pipeline jointly optimizes model compression and architecture for sub-50 ms inference, using latency feedback loops during NAS to meet strict runtime budgets.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the CASP14 protein dataset, CAMEO continuous evaluation dataset, and the ProteinNet benchmark for structure accuracy and generalization. Baselines include full AlphaFold2, AlphaFold-Multimer, OpenFold, and the previously published latency-optimized pruning + quantization method. Metrics: TM-score (target <1% absolute degradation from AlphaFold2), inference latency on Edge TPU and NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX (target <50 ms per protein), and peak memory usage (target <50% reduction vs baseline). Conduct 5 independent runs per configuration and perform ablation studies isolating pruning, quantization, knowledge distillation, and NAS components. Training compute budget will be limited to 100 A100 GPU hours, leveraging pretrained AlphaFold2 weights for distillation.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that applies fixed pruning and quantization ratios without architecture adaptation or knowledge transfer, our framework introduces dynamic NAS driven by hardware latency feedback combined with knowledge distillation to push latency below 50 ms on edge devices without compromising accuracy beyond 1% TM-score loss. This integrated, hardware-aware approach surpasses existing methods that either fail to meet real-time constraints or suffer from unacceptable accuracy drops under aggressive compression.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a diffusion-based generative framework that integrates sparse, noisy real-time single-molecule FRET and optical tweezer experimental data to iteratively predict dynamic protein folding pathways with uncertainty quantification, enabling accurate mechanistic modeling of folding kinetics beyond static structure prediction.

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art methods, including AlphaFold2 and fragment-based approaches like DeepFragLib, excel in predicting static native protein structures but do not model the dynamic folding process or incorporate live experimental feedback. The key open problem is accurately characterizing protein folding pathways and kinetics from incomplete, noisy single-molecule biophysical data, such as smFRET or optical tweezers, which are inherently sparse and uncertain. Without a robust generative model that can handle partial observations and quantify uncertainty in real time, mechanistic insights into folding intermediates, transition states, and misfolding remain inaccessible.

APPROACH:  
We propose a diffusion probabilistic model conditioned on multi-modal single-molecule experimental inputs (e.g., time-resolved smFRET distances, optical tweezer force-extension curves) that iteratively refines folding pathway ensembles. The model employs uncertainty-aware latent variable inference to handle noisy and partial data streams, dynamically updating folding trajectory distributions. Integration of experimental noise models and Bayesian filtering enables continuous feedback incorporation and posterior pathway estimation. Training leverages synthetic trajectories generated from molecular dynamics simulations with experimentally informed noise injection to bridge simulation-to-experiment gaps.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will use the Folding@home Protein Folding Kinetics Dataset, the smFRET Protein Folding Dataset by Lerner et al. (2018), and the Optical Tweezers Protein Unfolding Dataset from Bustamante Lab. Baselines include AlphaFold2, DeepFragLib, and the original diffusion folding pathway model without experimental conditioning. Metrics are pathway prediction accuracy measured by temporal RMSD to ground-truth folding trajectories (>10% absolute improvement targeted), uncertainty calibration evaluated by expected calibration error (<5% target), and runtime latency to support real-time feedback (<500 ms per update). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds and include ablations on noise modeling and multi-modal input integration.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike AlphaFold2 and DeepFragLib, which predict static end-state structures, our method explicitly models dynamic folding pathways conditioned on sparse, noisy single-molecule data streams, providing uncertainty-aware, real-time trajectory ensembles. Prior diffusion models for folding lack integration with live experimental feedback and uncertainty quantification, preventing iterative refinement under partial observations—our framework uniquely addresses these limitations to enable mechanistic biophysical insight into folding kinetics and misfolding mechanisms.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Develop a generative deep learning framework, RapidAgStruct, that predicts accurate antigen protein structures from incomplete or noisy sequence data within minutes, specifically optimized for emergent pathogen outbreak scenarios.

BACKGROUND: Current state-of-the-art protein structure predictors like AlphaFold2 require high-quality, full-length sequences and considerable inference times (minutes to hours), limiting their utility in urgent public health emergencies where pathogen sequences are partial, noisy, or rapidly evolving. This impedes timely antigen structural characterization critical for vaccine and therapeutic design in outbreak responses.

APPROACH: RapidAgStruct leverages a novel transformer-based generative model trained on a large corpus of antigen-related protein sequences and structures, incorporating a robust incomplete-sequence encoding scheme and uncertainty-aware latent representation to handle partial inputs. It integrates a multi-task learning objective combining structure prediction with sequence completion and antigenicity scoring. The model is optimized for sub-minute inference on GPU hardware by pruning and quantization techniques without sacrificing predictive accuracy.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the AntigenDB, PDBbind, and CASP-CAPRI datasets using TM-score, RMSD, and inference latency as metrics. Compare against AlphaFold2, RoseTTAFold, and ESMFold baselines. Target ≥10% improvement in inference speed (sub-minute runtime vs. ~5-10 min for AlphaFold2) with less than 5% TM-score degradation. Conduct five independent runs with ablation on incomplete input handling and multi-task objectives.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior methods that require near-complete sequences and extensive compute, RapidAgStruct directly models incomplete and noisy antigen sequences for rapid structure prediction, a capability not addressed by AlphaFold2, DeepTracer-LowResEnhance, or other SOTA tools, thereby enabling actionable structural insights under outbreak time constraints.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Bayesian deep generative model that integrates sparse, heterogeneous experimental data (Cryo-EM, NMR, and crosslinking mass spectrometry) with AlphaFold2 representations to produce uncertainty-aware, membrane protein structure predictions that explicitly quantify and reduce overconfidence in low-data regimes.

BACKGROUND:  
High-resolution structure prediction of membrane proteins remains challenging due to limited and noisy experimental data availability. Current approaches, including AlphaFold2 and recent Bayesian uncertainty-guided integrative methods, either rely on deterministic structural fusion or fail to jointly model multiple heterogeneous data types with calibrated uncertainty, leading to overconfident predictions and inaccurate models when experimental information is sparse or incomplete.

APPROACH:  
We propose to build a Bayesian hierarchical deep generative framework that conditions on AlphaFold2 embeddings along with sparse Cryo-EM density maps, NMR chemical shifts, and crosslinking mass spectrometry constraints to jointly infer a posterior distribution over membrane protein conformations. The model will explicitly represent experimental noise and propagate uncertainty through a variational inference scheme that balances data fidelity with prior learned from AlphaFold2. A key component is a multi-modal uncertainty calibration module that reconciles conflicting evidence across modalities, enabling robust integrative modeling that quantifies confidence at residue-level resolution.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on established membrane protein benchmarks: the Membrane Protein Data Bank (MPDB), the Cryo-EM Model Challenge dataset, and the NMR Lipid-Associated Protein Dataset (NMR-LAPD). Baselines include AlphaFold2, DeepTracer-LowResEnhance, and the Bayesian Uncertainty-Guided Integration method from the tournament winner. Metrics will measure RMSD improvement (>0.5 Å absolute reduction), correlation of predicted uncertainty with empirical error (>15% increase in confidence-accuracy correlation), and structural ensemble diversity (Shannon entropy). Experiments will use three random seeds with ablation studies on each data modality and uncertainty calibration. Training will utilize ~100 A100 GPU hours.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods, our approach jointly models multiple sparse experimental data types with AlphaFold2 priors in a unified Bayesian framework that explicitly quantifies and calibrates uncertainty at residue-level, enabling more accurate and confidence-calibrated membrane protein models in severely data-limited contexts—a capability absent from deterministic or single-modality fusion methods.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an FPGA-accelerated ultra-low latency noise-adaptive quantum error correction framework that dynamically tunes low-overhead QEC codes on NISQ devices in real time, achieving sub-5 microsecond correction update latencies to significantly reduce logical error rates under rapidly varying noise conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
NISQ devices suffer from rapidly fluctuating qubit noise and gate errors that degrade logical qubit fidelity. Current low-latency adaptive correction methods, such as FPGA-accelerated noise profiling with static QEC code parameters, fail to keep pace with fast noise dynamics, resulting in outdated error correction and limited error suppression. Furthermore, existing low-overhead QEC codes for NISQ devices cannot adapt their parameters on-the-fly due to classical controller latency constraints, leaving a critical gap in real-time, fine-grained noise mitigation.

APPROACH:  
We propose a tightly integrated hardware-software co-designed system that uses FPGA-accelerated noise profiling combined with a dynamic QEC code parameter tuning engine to continuously optimize error correction codes (e.g., code distance, syndrome extraction frequency, decoding thresholds) based on sub-microsecond noise measurements. The FPGA performs real-time noise characterization on superconducting qubit hardware, feeds this data into an adaptive QEC parameter optimizer embedded on the FPGA fabric, which updates QEC control parameters in under 5 microseconds. This enables rapid adaptation to non-stationary noise without qubit overhead increase, employing low-overhead codes such as the recently developed NISQ-tailored QLDPC variants. The system supports closed-loop operation by continuously validating logical error rates from injected noise benchmarks and adjusting control parameters accordingly.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on superconducting qubit noise datasets including IBM Quantum Experience noise traces, Rigetti Aspen QPU noise injection benchmarks, and Google Sycamore calibration data. Compare against baselines: (1) static low-overhead QEC codes from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” (2026), (2) FPGA-accelerated adaptive correction from “Ultra-low latency noise profiling via FPGA-accelerated adaptive correction” (tournament winner), and (3) compiler-level noise mitigation from “Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design” (2026). Metrics: reduce logical error rates by >30% relative to baseline static QEC under dynamic noise, achieve QEC parameter update latency below 5 μs (vs. 10 μs baseline), and maintain overall system overhead below 15% qubit increase. Experimental protocol includes 10 independent runs per noise scenario with ablations on update frequency, code types, and FPGA resource allocation, conducted on a hybrid quantum-classical testbed with 100 FPGA runtime hours.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior FPGA-accelerated adaptive correction methods that only update fixed QEC parameters with ~10 μs latency, our approach dynamically tunes QEC code parameters in hardware with sub-5 μs latency, enabling real-time closed-loop optimization of low-overhead codes tailored to rapid noise fluctuations. This hardware-software co-design bridges the gap between static QEC code deployment and dynamic noise conditions unaddressed by prior work, preserving logical qubit fidelity without qubit overhead increase, and thus significantly advancing practical quantum error correction for NISQ devices.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an on-device, ultra-low-latency adaptive quantum error correction framework that integrates real-time Bayesian noise profile estimation with dynamic, hardware-aware low-overhead QEC code reconfiguration to enhance logical qubit fidelity in mobile NISQ devices.

BACKGROUND:  
NISQ devices suffer from highly variable and context-dependent noise profiles, especially in mobile or dynamic environments, which rapidly degrade quantum error correction (QEC) efficacy. Existing adaptive noise estimation methods (e.g., batch processing or cloud-offloading) incur inference latencies exceeding control cycle times, leading to outdated models and suboptimal error mitigation. Furthermore, current low-overhead QEC codes lack dynamic adaptability to continuously changing noise characteristics, limiting logical error rate improvements.

APPROACH:  
We propose a tightly integrated system that performs sub-millisecond, on-device Bayesian updating of noise profiles using real-time quantum noise trace datasets (e.g., from IBM Quantum Experience and Rigetti Aspen operating under mobile conditions). These noise estimates drive a lightweight hardware-aware QEC code reconfiguration engine, which dynamically adjusts the parameters and code selection (e.g., switching between tailored low-overhead codes such as the Bacon-Shor code and the recently proposed low-qubit surface variants) to optimize error correction performance under current noise conditions. The entire pipeline runs within a 1 ms control loop latency on embedded classical processors co-located with the quantum hardware, enabling immediate and continuous error correction adaptation.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted using the IBM Quantum Noise Dataset (QND 2023), Rigetti Aspen Noise Trace Dataset, and the Google Sycamore Device Characterization Dataset under simulated mobile environmental noise conditions. Baselines for comparison include: (1) Low-Overhead QEC Codes for NISQ Devices (2026), (2) Low-Latency Adaptive Noise Profile Estimation for Mobile NISQ Devices (the tournament winner), and (3) Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design (2026). Metrics will include logical qubit error rate reduction (target >30% relative improvement), average gate fidelity increase (target >5% absolute), and control loop latency (maintain <1 ms). The experimental protocol involves 10 independent runs per dataset condition, ablation studies on Bayesian updating frequency and QEC reconfiguration granularity, using quantum simulators accelerated on NVIDIA A100 GPUs and embedded-class ARM processors for latency profiling.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, which either focuses solely on noise profile estimation without integrated dynamic QEC adaptation or relies on static low-overhead codes, our method provides a unified, on-device low-latency Bayesian noise estimator coupled directly with a hardware-aware dynamic QEC code reconfiguration mechanism. This enables real-time, context-aware error correction optimization, a capability absent in current batch or cloud-offloading approaches and static QEC frameworks, thus significantly advancing practical quantum error resilience in mobile NISQ settings.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a real-time, hardware-specific quantum error-aware compilation framework that integrates dynamic noise-adaptive heuristics with online calibration data to achieve sub-millisecond compilation latency and reduce logical error rates by at least 15% on NISQ devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum error correction (QEC) methods for NISQ devices face a critical bottleneck: while tailored compilation and error-aware heuristics exist, they typically operate offline with static noise models, causing compilation latencies that are too high (>1 ms) for live error mitigation during quantum executions. This latency prevents effective feedback and adaptation to rapidly fluctuating noise profiles, limiting real-time error suppression and coherence preservation essential for practical NISQ computation at scale.

APPROACH:  
The method leverages a modular compilation pipeline that incorporates hardware-specific noise characterization updates from online calibration routines (e.g., IBM Quantum’s Qiskit Pulse calibration logs and Rigetti’s Quantum Cloud Services noise profiles) to dynamically adapt qubit mapping, gate scheduling, and error-avoidance routing heuristics in real time. A lightweight heuristic engine, optimized via reinforcement learning on initial noise profiles from datasets such as IBM Quantum’s Transmon Noise Dataset and Rigetti Aspen-11 Noise Logs, enables sub-millisecond compile times measured on classical FPGA-accelerated controllers. The system continuously refines compilation strategies within live quantum control loops to minimize logical error rates on benchmark circuits from the Quantum Volume 32 and QASMBench suites.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on IBM Quantum’s Transmon Noise Dataset, Rigetti Aspen-11 Noise Logs, and Google Sycamore Noise Profiles using benchmark circuits from Quantum Volume 32 and QASMBench. Compare against baselines including Qiskit’s standard transpiler, t|ket>’s noise-aware compiler, and the Low-latency error-aware compilation method (the tournament winner). Metrics include compilation latency (target <1 ms), logical error rates (target >15% reduction), and fidelity improvement (>5% absolute increase). Experiments will run on FPGA-accelerated classical control hardware with 10 independent random seeds per benchmark and ablations isolating the impact of online noise adaptation and heuristic optimization.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike existing work that relies on static or generically tuned heuristics and offline compilation, this approach uniquely integrates live hardware noise calibration data into a real-time, ultra-low-latency compilation pipeline, enabling dynamic error-aware decision-making during quantum operation. This enables error suppression beyond prior art (>15% logical error reduction at <1 ms latency), a capability currently unachieved by state-of-the-art quantum compilers and error correction frameworks.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Hybrid quantum error correction integrating lightweight on-chip error detection with adaptive, ultra-low-latency ML post-processing pipelines optimized via reinforcement learning to dynamically balance logical error rate reduction against NISQ device latency constraints.

BACKGROUND: Current NISQ devices suffer from high logical error rates due to decoherence and gate noise, yet implementing full quantum error correction (QEC) codes is prohibitive due to qubit overhead and latency constraints. Hybrid error mitigation schemes either impose significant quantum circuit complexity or rely on slow classical inference, which fails to meet strict real-time latency requirements of many NISQ applications, leaving a critical gap in scalable, practical error correction.

APPROACH: We propose a hybrid QEC framework that combines compact, lightweight on-chip error detection circuits producing syndrome data at minimal quantum resource cost with a reinforcement learning–guided classical ML inference pipeline optimized for ultra-low latency. The RL agent dynamically selects and tunes ML classifiers (e.g., quantized neural networks, decision trees) based on real-time device noise profiles and latency budgets, enabling adaptive post-processing error correction that minimizes logical errors while strictly adhering to latency constraints on hardware such as superconducting qubit platforms or trapped ions.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark NISQ quantum output datasets including IBM Quantum Experience QASM noise simulator outputs, Rigetti Aspen-11 QPU raw measurement data, and Google Sycamore calibration runs; compare against baselines including the original “Hybrid Error Mitigation Combining Lightweight On-Chip Detection and ML Post-Processing” method, surface code QEC implementations, and the low-overhead QEC framework from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices.” Metrics include logical error rate reduction (>20% improvement over baseline hybrid methods), latency overhead reduction (target <10% increase over raw circuit execution time), and ML inference accuracy (>95%) under end-to-end latency constraints. Conduct ablation studies on ML model complexity, reinforcement learning policy effectiveness, and varying noise conditions with 5 randomized seeds per configuration on superconducting qubit emulators and available cloud QPUs.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior hybrid schemes that use fixed ML classifiers or static error detection circuits, this approach introduces reinforcement learning to adaptively optimize ML post-processing under strict latency constraints and dynamic noise conditions, enabling real-time, scalable error correction on NISQ devices without incurring prohibitive quantum or classical resource costs. This dynamic adaptability and latency-aware ML integration is beyond capabilities of existing static hybrid or low-overhead QEC methods.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Embed multi-scale longitudinal noise trends into adaptive, differentiable quantum error correction code parameterizers to enable proactive, predictive error mitigation and logical fidelity improvement on NISQ devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum error correction (QEC) methods for NISQ devices struggle to adapt proactively to evolving noise profiles due to their reactive tuning based on short-term or instantaneous noise measurements. This leads to suboptimal logical qubit fidelity and limits fault tolerance scalability under long-term device drift and correlated noise phenomena. Existing frameworks such as longitudinal noise trend embedding (LNT-E) have not yet integrated multi-scale temporal patterns or combined them with adaptive parameter tuning in a differentiable framework that supports scalable multi-parameter optimization.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical noise trend embedding framework that encodes multi-scale (hourly, daily, weekly) noise characteristics from long-duration device noise logs (e.g., IBM Quantum Experience and Rigetti Aspen datasets) into compact latent representations. These embeddings feed into a differentiable code parameter optimizer integrated with error correction decoders (e.g., MWPM-based surface code decoders and neural QEC decoders) to perform proactive, continuous multi-parameter tuning of code distance, syndrome extraction frequency, and decoder thresholds. The system leverages recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and attention mechanisms to capture temporal dependencies and noise drift, enabling anticipatory adjustments that improve logical error rates. Training uses supervised fine-tuning on real noise data with reinforcement learning components to maximize logical fidelity over time.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on the following publicly available noise datasets:  
- IBM Quantum Experience multi-month noise logs (2023–2024)  
- Rigetti Aspen device noise characterization logs (2023)  
- Google Sycamore noise calibration data (2024)  

Baselines for comparison include:  
- Longitudinal noise trend embedding (LNT-E) without multi-scale or hierarchical embedding  
- Standard surface code with fixed code parameters and MWPM decoding  
- Neural decoder-based QEC without noise trend embedding  

Metrics will be:  
- Logical error rate reduction (target >30% absolute reduction vs LNT-E baseline)  
- Prediction accuracy of noise trend embeddings (target >85% accuracy on next-day noise forecasts)  
- Improvement in quantum volume equivalents (measured by logical qubit fidelity increase >10%)  

The experimental protocol includes training over 500 GPU-hours on NVIDIA A100s, using 5 random seeds, with ablations to isolate the effect of multi-scale embedding, RNN vs transformer encoders, and reinforcement learning tuning components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work (including the baseline LNT-E), this method uniquely incorporates hierarchical multi-scale temporal noise embeddings into a differentiable quantum error correction parameter optimizer that jointly tunes multiple code parameters proactively rather than reactively. This enables predictive and anticipatory QEC code adaptation to long-term noise evolution patterns, a capability not demonstrated by existing single-scale or static QEC tuning frameworks.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA: Develop explicit scaling laws that jointly model parameter count, domain-specific vocabulary rarity, and label scarcity to precisely predict performance gains for large language models adapted to specialized scientific domains.

BACKGROUND: Current scaling laws for large language models (LLMs) do not account for the presence of sparse, domain-specific tokens and limited labeled data typical in scientific text adaptation, leading to inaccurate performance and sample-efficiency predictions. This impairs practical decisions on model sizing and training resource allocation when adapting LLMs to specialized fields such as biomedical and physics literature, where rare terminology and label scarcity dominate.

APPROACH: We propose a novel parametric scaling law framework that incorporates vocabulary rarity metrics (e.g., token frequency distribution skewness) and label availability alongside model size and compute into a unified predictive formula. The method models the interaction effects between rare token representation capacity and limited supervision, enabling better estimation of performance (accuracy, perplexity) improvements when increasing model parameters or fine-tuning data in domain-specific contexts. This framework will be validated on fine-tuning and few-shot adaptation scenarios for domain-adapted LLMs.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on biomedical QA dataset PubMedQA, physics abstracts from arXiv, and chemistry patent text from USPTO, benchmarking against GPT-3, BioBERT, and GPT-4 adapted variants. Metrics include domain-specific accuracy improvements (+3% absolute target), perplexity reduction below 20 on scientific text, and sample efficiency measured as performance gain per labeled example. Experiments will use consistent training budgets on 8x A100 GPUs, with 5 random seeds per setting and ablations isolating vocabulary rarity and label scarcity components.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior scaling laws that treat data and vocabulary as homogeneous or ignore label scarcity, this approach explicitly models and quantifies how rare domain-specific vocabulary and limited labeled data jointly determine scaling benefits—enabling precise adaptation sizing predictions unattainable with current methods.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Adaptive Capacity Scaling for Large Language Models via Entropy-Regularized Parameter Allocation to Dynamically Optimize Compute Efficiency Across Linguistic Subdomains.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for large language models (LLMs) primarily treat model capacity allocation as static and monolithic, ignoring the varying complexity and information density across different linguistic subdomains (e.g., syntax, semantics, pragmatics). This often leads to inefficient parameter usage, where some parts of the model overfit simpler subdomains while others underfit complex ones. The open problem is how to dynamically allocate capacity within an LLM’s architecture during training to optimize overall sample and compute efficiency while improving downstream generalization.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel method that integrates cross-component entropy regularization into the training objective to guide an adaptive reallocation of model parameters and compute budget across distinct linguistic submodules (e.g., attention heads or feedforward layers specialized for different tasks). This entropy-based feedback dynamically measures uncertainty and information complexity in each subdomain, enabling the model to upweight capacity where uncertainty is highest and downscale it where redundancy is detected. The method builds on a meta-learning framework where parameter allocation is updated online via a differentiable entropy regularizer applied at intermediate model layers, facilitating efficient scaling that respects linguistic structure.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on three benchmarks: GLUE for natural language understanding, SQuAD 2.0 for question answering, and WikiText-103 for language modeling. Baselines include GPT-4, BERT-large, and the Adaptive Modality Balancing method from the tournament winner. Metrics will be: GLUE average accuracy (target +3% absolute improvement), SQuAD 2.0 F1 score (target +2% absolute improvement), and WikiText-103 perplexity (target <18). Experiments will run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs over 3 random seeds per setting, with ablations on the entropy regularization strength and layer-wise capacity gating.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior scaling laws or static capacity allocation methods, our approach dynamically redistributes parameters within the LLM during training based on entropy-derived uncertainty signals specific to linguistic subdomains, enabling fine-grained architectural adaptation. This goes beyond the static modality balancing in the tournament winner and general scaling laws focused on overall model size or data quantity, addressing a fundamental inefficiency in current LLM scaling by leveraging internal linguistic complexity metrics for adaptive capacity scaling.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop and validate an adaptive, topic- and informativeness-guided sampling strategy that dynamically prioritizes underrepresented scientific subdomains during large-scale LLM pretraining to improve scaling efficiency and downstream extraction recall in heterogeneous biomedical corpora.

BACKGROUND:  
Current large language model (LLM) scaling laws assume uniform or frequency-based sampling of pretraining data, which leads to overfitting on dominant topics and poor generalization to rare but critical scientific subdomains. This sampling bias limits data efficiency and downstream task recall, especially in heterogeneous and scarce corpora such as biomedical literature. The open problem is designing sampling policies that improve scaling behavior by better balancing coverage across diverse scientific topics without increasing the overall training compute.

APPROACH:  
The method integrates unsupervised topic modeling (e.g., BERTopic or Scientific Topic Embeddings) with informativeness metrics derived from uncertainty and novelty (e.g., entropy of model predictions, gradient-based informativeness) to construct a dynamic sampling policy. This policy adaptively selects text segments from a large biomedical corpus (e.g., PubMed Central Open Access and the COVID-19 Open Research Dataset) to maximize coverage of underrepresented subdomains and high-value content while reducing redundant sampling. The approach continuously updates sampling distributions during training to balance exploitation of well-learned topics and exploration of rare topics, thereby improving data efficiency and downstream recall.

EXPERIMENT:  
Train transformer-based LLMs (comparable to GPT-2 and BioBERT architectures) on biomedical corpora including PubMed Central Open Access, COVID-19 Open Research Dataset, and the BioASQ QA dataset. Baselines include uniform sampling, frequency-based sampling, and the Adaptive Sampling method from “Adaptive Sampling Strategies for Domain-Specific Scientific Text Scaling.” Evaluation metrics are: (1) downstream extraction F1 score on BioASQ and BC5CDR entity recognition tasks with target >7% absolute F1 gains over uniform baseline, (2) perplexity on held-out biomedical text with target reduction >5 perplexity points, and (3) training sample efficiency, measured by % reduction in total tokens needed to achieve target F1. Experiments run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 3 random seeds each, including ablations disabling topic modeling or informativeness components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that employs static or heuristic sampling, this approach dynamically integrates topic modeling and quantifiable informativeness metrics in a closed-loop adaptive sampling system that explicitly targets scaling laws for data efficiency in heterogeneous scientific corpora. It goes beyond the “Adaptive Sampling Strategies” baseline by formalizing and empirically validating the interplay between topic diversity and informativeness in large-scale LLM training, enabling provable recall improvements and reduced sample complexity not addressed in existing scaling laws or pretraining data selection analyses.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a principled framework integrating synthetic data augmentation quality metrics into scaling laws to adaptively adjust model size and training compute for low-resource languages, thereby stabilizing training and improving downstream translation quality.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for large language models (LLMs) do not adequately model the effects of synthetic data augmentation, especially in low-resource language scenarios where training data is artificially expanded. This omission leads to unreliable capacity predictions and training instability due to unmodeled distribution shifts and variable data quality in augmented corpora. Effectively quantifying and incorporating augmentation-induced shifts into scaling laws is an open problem that limits the practical scalability and efficiency of LLMs trained on synthetic datasets.

APPROACH:  
We propose to extend classical scaling laws by introducing parameters that explicitly quantify synthetic data augmentation quality, measured via distributional divergence (e.g., Fréchet Distance) and informativeness metrics (e.g., mutual information with real data). These parameters will be incorporated into a modified scaling law formulation that predicts optimal model size, data volume, and compute budget, adapting dynamically to augmentation quality. This framework will be validated by training Transformer-based LLMs on augmented low-resource corpora, where synthetic data is generated by state-of-the-art back-translation and language model-based augmentation techniques, enabling adaptive scaling strategies that optimize BLEU and COMET scores while avoiding training collapse.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on FLORES-200 (low-resource multilingual translation), WMT19 Nepali-English, and IWSLT 2017 Telugu-English translation datasets augmented with synthetic corpora generated via MarianMT back-translation and GPT-4 augmentation. Baselines include Transformer baseline (Vaswani et al. 2017), mBART50, and FAIRSEQ augmented training without adaptive scaling. Metrics: BLEU (target >2 points improvement over augmentation-agnostic scaling), COMET (target >5% relative gain), and training stability measured by loss variance and early training divergence frequency reduction (>30% fewer instability events). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 clusters with a compute budget capped at 500k GPU hours, with 3 seeds per setting and ablations on augmentation quality parameters.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that treats augmented data as homogeneous or ignores augmentation quality in scaling laws, this method explicitly models data distribution shifts induced by synthetic augmentation within the scaling law framework, enabling principled adaptive scaling. This surpasses previous approaches such as "Robust Scaling Strategies Leveraging Synthetic Data Augmentation," which do not quantitatively integrate augmentation quality metrics into capacity prediction, and inverse problem formulations that do not specifically address synthetic data quality effects on scaling dynamics.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate adaptive gradient compression with dynamic parameter expansion schedules guided by learned scaling laws to enable efficient training of ultra-large language models beyond current communication bottlenecks without accuracy loss.

BACKGROUND:  
Current large language models face severe communication overhead during distributed training as parameter counts scale, limiting practical model size and training speed. Existing methods like Scalable Parameter Expansion with Gradient Compression reduce communication but rely on fixed compression schemes and static parameter growth schedules, which do not optimally exploit emerging scaling laws that relate model size, data efficiency, and compute. There is an open problem in jointly optimizing parameter expansion and communication compression dynamically during training, guided by data-driven scaling laws, to maximize training efficiency while preserving model quality.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel framework, Dynamic Scaling-Driven Gradient Compression (DSGC), which couples adaptive gradient compression algorithms (e.g., gradient sparsification with learned quantization thresholds) with parameter expansion schedules dynamically modulated by observed scaling law trends during training. The system continuously estimates effective scaling exponents from intermediate losses and adjusts both compression parameters and model width/depth increments in real time, minimizing communication while maintaining gradient fidelity. This joint optimization is implemented atop TPU pod-based distributed training, leveraging reinforcement learning to control compression and expansion policies.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate DSGC on large-scale language modeling using C4, WikiText-103, and The Pile datasets, training models ranging from 1B to 100B parameters. Baselines will include Scalable Parameter Expansion with Gradient Compression (SPEG), GPT-3, and AdamW-optimized Transformer models. Metrics will measure perplexity (target <15 on WikiText-103), training speedup (aiming for >30% reduction in wall-clock time vs SPEG), and final zero-shot downstream task accuracy on SuperGLUE (seeking >2% absolute improvement over SPEG). Experiments will run on TPU v4 pods with 3 seeds per configuration and include ablations on compression granularity and expansion schedule responsiveness.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, DSGC jointly learns scaling laws online during training to drive both gradient compression and parameter expansion adaptively, whereas SPEG uses fixed schedules and static compression. This dynamic coupling meaningfully exceeds current methods by enabling larger models to be trained more efficiently without sacrificing accuracy, breaking the communication bottleneck in distributed LLM scaling.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a real-time, multi-agent causal discovery framework with safe extrapolation capabilities that integrates counterfactual reasoning and domain adaptation techniques to enable robust autonomous vehicle simulation under unseen driving conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current causal discovery methods from observational data are limited in their ability to model complex, nonlinear multi-agent interactions dynamically and fail to generalize safely beyond observed scenarios, which is critical for autonomous vehicle testing and deployment. Additionally, existing approaches struggle with real-time inference and counterfactual reasoning necessary to simulate intervention scenarios in interactive environments with many agents. This research addresses the gap of enabling fast, accurate causal inference that supports safe extrapolation and counterfactual simulation in high-dimensional, dynamic driving contexts.

APPROACH:  
We propose Multi-Agent Causal Discovery with Safe Extrapolation (MACD-SE), a framework combining nonlinear causal structure learning via an extension of KiNCD with multi-agent interaction graphs learned using attention mechanisms to capture dynamic dependencies. MACD-SE incorporates domain-adaptive invariant risk minimization to ensure causal models generalize safely to unseen driving conditions. Counterfactual reasoning modules leverage learned causal graphs for real-time intervention scenario generation. A lightweight continuous optimization algorithm with GPU acceleration enables inference within sub-second timeframes to meet real-time constraints.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate MACD-SE on benchmark datasets including CARLA Simulator logs, the Waymo Open Dataset, and the NGSIM vehicle trajectory dataset. Compare against baselines KiNCD, S-FCI, and Orthogonal Structure Search (OSS) on metrics:  
- Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) for causal graph accuracy (target >10% improvement over baselines),  
- Counterfactual prediction error (MSE reduction >15%),  
- Real-time inference latency (<500 ms per scenario),  
- Generalization robustness quantified by performance drop across domain shifts (target <5% degradation).  
Run experiments on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of 48 hours per dataset, averaging results over 5 random seeds. Conduct ablation studies on domain adaptation and multi-agent graph components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works such as KiNCD and S-FCI that focus on static or partially nonlinear causal discovery without domain adaptation, MACD-SE uniquely integrates safe extrapolation to unseen environments, real-time counterfactual reasoning, and explicit modeling of dynamic multi-agent interactions, enabling rigorous autonomous vehicle testing beyond observed data distributions.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA: Develop a lightweight, adaptive causal representation learning framework that performs continuous causal discovery on streaming, non-stationary observational data directly on resource-constrained edge IoT devices without requiring costly retraining.

BACKGROUND: Existing causal discovery methods, including KiNCD and S-FCI, rely on heavy computational resources and often assume stationary data distributions or require offline retraining when distributions shift, making them unsuitable for real-time causal inference on edge IoT devices with limited memory and compute. Moreover, current methods do not support continuous model adaptation to non-stationary streaming data, limiting their applicability in ubiquitous IoT settings where causal structures evolve over time and timely decisions are critical.

APPROACH: We propose an edge-optimized causal representation learning system employing a modular, lightweight neural architecture with incremental causal graph updates driven by streaming data using efficient online optimization and adaptive forgetting mechanisms to handle concept drift. The model integrates a compact Kolmogorov-Arnold Network-inspired causal function approximator with sparse orthogonal structure search to reduce model complexity and inference latency. Additionally, we leverage a novel causal memory replay buffer selectively retaining influential past samples to stabilize causal structure updates without full retraining.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on real-world benchmark datasets including the CauseMe benchmark suite (CauseMe-10 and CauseMe-20) and the DREAM4 multifactorial gene expression datasets, comparing against KiNCD, S-FCI, and amortized causal discovery baselines. Metrics include Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) with a target reduction of ≥3 points, F1-score improvements on causal edge identification by ≥5%, and runtime latency reduction of at least 40% on edge-like hardware (e.g., Raspberry Pi 4). Experimental protocol involves 5 random seeds, ablations on replay buffer size and forgetting rate, with training budgets limited to 2 hours on a single ARM CPU core to simulate edge constraints.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior approaches which require offline batch retraining or do not scale to constrained devices, our method uniquely enables continuous, online causal discovery from streaming, non-stationary data directly on edge IoT hardware through a combination of lightweight modular causal representation, sparse orthogonal structure search, and adaptive memory replay, achieving real-time adaptability and significantly reduced computational overhead.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an Incremental Nonlinear Causal Discovery framework using adaptive Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks with latency-aware online estimators tailored for real-time high-frequency financial streaming data under 10ms latency constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current causal discovery methods either rely on batch processing or do not scale well to high-dimensional nonlinear dependencies under hard latency constraints, making them unsuitable for fast-paced financial markets. Existing incremental or streaming causal methods lack the ability to simultaneously handle nonlinearities, non-stationarity, and ultra-low latency demands (<10ms) on high-frequency trading data streams. This gap prevents timely, accurate causal insights necessary for automated trading and risk management.

APPROACH:  
We propose Incremental KAN-Causal Discovery (IKAN-CD), which integrates an online, adaptive Kolmogorov-Arnold Network-based nonlinear function approximator with incremental information-theoretic causality estimators. IKAN-CD incorporates an adaptive windowing mechanism that dynamically adjusts the data horizon to maintain robustness to non-stationarity while meeting strict latency budgets. The algorithm processes NASDAQ ITCH and LOBSTER limit order book streams in real-time, incrementally updating causal graphs with streaming data features, exploiting parallel GPU computation and efficient memory use to ensure sub-10ms end-to-end latency.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate IKAN-CD on NASDAQ ITCH (high-frequency order book data), LOBSTER (limit order book data for multiple stocks), and the UCI Gas Sensor Array Drift Dataset (non-financial nonlinear streaming data). Compare against Incremental Information-Theoretic Causal Discovery (baseline from winning direction), KiNCD (KAN-based offline nonlinear causal discovery), and S-FCI (constraint-based causal discovery across environments). Metrics will include causal discovery accuracy (F1-score improvement >3% over IKAN-CD baseline), latency (reduce below 10ms vs 20ms for baselines), and robustness to concept drift (maintain >90% precision under non-stationarity). Conduct ablation studies on windowing strategies and nonlinear approximation components. Experiments run on an NVIDIA A100 GPU with 5 random seeds each.

NOVELTY:  
This work is the first to combine online incremental nonlinear causal discovery via Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks with latency-aware adaptive windowing to achieve sub-10ms latency on real-world high-frequency financial data streams. Unlike KiNCD and prior nonlinear methods that require batch or offline optimization, IKAN-CD operates fully incrementally with theoretical latency guarantees. This extends the winning direction by not only minimizing latency but also enabling nonlinear causal inference at scale and robustness in streaming non-stationary environments unseen in prior work.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an adaptive, online causal discovery framework that jointly models nonlinear, high-dimensional dependencies and latent confounders under rapid, non-stationary regime shifts by integrating KAN-based nonlinear causal discovery with invariant prediction and multi-environment latent confounder adjustment in streaming financial and healthcare data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current real-time causal discovery methods for non-stationary streaming data, such as "Real-Time Invariant Causal Prediction under Non-Stationary Financial Streams," assume stationary or slowly changing latent confounding and often handle linear or low-dimensional relationships, limiting robustness in adversarial environments with abrupt regime shifts and complex nonlinear dependencies. Moreover, no existing method combines efficient nonlinear causal discovery, latent confounder adjustment, and real-time change-point detection in a unified streaming framework, which is critical for reliable causal inference in volatile domains like financial markets or medical monitoring.

APPROACH:  
We propose Real-Time Kernel-Integrated Latent-Invariant Causal Discovery (RT-KILICD), which integrates: (1) a kernelized Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) module for modeling complex nonlinear causal relationships in high-dimensional streaming data; (2) an online multi-environment latent confounder adjustment mechanism inspired by S-FCI that uses interventional and observational shifts detected via an adaptive change-point detector optimized for streaming data; and (3) invariant causal predictor set updates that leverage continual distribution shifts to robustly identify stable causal parents across regimes. The system continuously updates causal graphs and predictor sets with low latency, leveraging efficient kernel approximations and a novel streaming constraint solver for latent confounder inference, enabling robust causal discovery and prediction in the presence of adversarial non-stationarity.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate RT-KILICD on the NYSE Limit Order Book dataset and the MIMIC-III clinical ICU dataset, augmented with synthetic regime shifts and latent confounding scenarios. Baselines include Real-Time Invariant Causal Prediction (RT-ICP), KiNCD, and S-FCI. Metrics: (1) causal graph F1-score and SHD (structural hamming distance) with >5% absolute improvement over baselines; (2) detection latency below 50 milliseconds per regime shift, reducing baseline latency by at least 30%; (3) stability of causal predictors measured by invariant risk minimization error reduced by >10%. Run experiments on a 4x NVIDIA A100 GPU setup over 5 random seeds, including ablations disabling latent confounder adjustment and nonlinear kernel components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike RT-ICP, which assumes known causal sets and linearity under stationarity, RT-KILICD jointly infers nonlinear causal structures and latent confounders in real time under adversarial regime shifts. Differing from KiNCD and S-FCI, which operate offline or on static datasets, our method uniquely integrates kernelized nonlinear discovery, online latent confounder adjustment, and adaptive invariant prediction in a streaming setting, enabling low-latency, robust causal discovery in complex, high-dimensional, and rapidly changing real-world environments.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Bayesian-symbolic causal discovery framework that jointly models heterogeneous noise, missingness, and nonlinear dependencies in limited clinical observational data using uncertainty-aware rule induction combined with deep nonlinear function approximators and multi-environment invariance constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current causal discovery approaches on observational clinical data fail to reliably handle heterogeneous noise, missing data, and nonlinear causal relationships simultaneously, leading to unstable causal rules that clinicians distrust. Existing symbolic causal rule learners lack deep modeling of nonlinearities and do not exploit multi-environment invariance to enhance robustness under limited, noisy observations. There is a need for a method that explicitly quantifies uncertainty and integrates nonlinear causal functional forms while pruning and stabilizing symbolic causal rules for clinical interpretability.

APPROACH:  
We propose Robust Bayesian Symbolic Causal Rule Learning (RB-SCRL), which combines: (1) a hierarchical Bayesian noise and missingness model tailored to clinical heterogeneity for probabilistic uncertainty quantification; (2) a nonlinear causal discovery backbone based on Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) to flexibly model complex causal mechanisms; (3) symbolic causal rule induction with uncertainty-weighted pruning to retain highly stable and interpretable causal rules; and (4) multi-environment invariance constraints leveraging heterogeneous clinical settings in eICU and MIMIC-IV to identify robust causal relations unaffected by confounding. This approach integrates continuous optimization over nonlinear functional forms with discrete symbolic rule search under a unified probabilistic framework.

EXPERIMENT:  
Validate RB-SCRL on the eICU Collaborative Research Database, MIMIC-IV, and the NHANES clinical dataset, comparing against baselines including Robust Symbolic Causal Rule Learning (the winning method), KiNCD, S-FCI, and Orthogonal Structure Search. Evaluate rule stability improvements (>15% stability gain), clinical outcome prediction accuracy (AUC improvement >3%), and robustness to synthetic noise injection (maintain >90% rule fidelity under 20% noise). Perform ablation studies on Bayesian noise modeling, nonlinear KAN backbone, and multi-environment invariance constraints. Use 5 random seeds, train on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget capped at 2 weeks.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD and prior symbolic rule learners, RB-SCRL unifies nonlinear causal function modeling with Bayesian uncertainty-aware symbolic rule induction and explicitly leverages multi-environment data to isolate invariant causal relationships under noise and missingness. Prior methods do not jointly address uncertainty quantification, nonlinear causal functions, symbolic rule stability, and multi-environment invariance in a single framework, which is critical for reliable clinical causal discovery from limited observational data.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an ultra-low-voltage, variability-aware analog in-memory computing (AIMC) architecture incorporating adaptive noise-mitigation and error-correcting feedback loops specifically optimized for resource-constrained edge IoT neuromorphic processors.

BACKGROUND:  
Current analog in-memory computing solutions for edge IoT devices suffer from severe analog noise and device variability at ultra-low voltage operation, which drastically reduces energy efficiency and inference accuracy. Existing neuromorphic approaches either compromise scaling to strict power and area constraints or degrade robustness against analog non-idealities, preventing practical deployment in highly resource-limited edge scenarios.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid circuit-algorithm co-design integrating: (1) ultra-low-voltage AIMC arrays using novel device-level variability-tolerant transistor designs and compact memory cells; (2) adaptive analog noise-shaping and on-chip calibration loops that dynamically track and correct device drifts and noise in real time; and (3) lightweight error-correcting feedback mechanisms employing sparsity-aware coding schemes tailored for neuromorphic inference workloads. This system runs at sub-0.5V supply with minimal overhead, enabling robust energy-efficient neuromorphic computing on edge IoT platforms.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on neuromorphic vision and speech benchmarks including ImageNet-1K (for visual classification), Speech Commands Dataset v2 (for keyword spotting), and DVS Gesture Dataset (for event-based sensing). Baselines include: (1) ultra-low-power analog AIMC from “Ultra-Low-Voltage Analog In-Memory Computing for Edge IoT Devices” (tournament winner), (2) ferroelectric synaptic transistor DropConnect implementation, and (3) the vertical dual-gate neurotransistor EGT device. Metrics: achieve >3% absolute top-1 accuracy improvement on ImageNet-1K vs baseline AIMC under 0.5V operation, reduce energy per inference by ≥25%, and decrease inference latency by ≥15%. Experimental protocol includes full system simulation on cycle-accurate AIMC hardware models, 5 random seeds per dataset, and ablation studies on noise mitigation and error correction modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that either tolerate noise passively or trade off energy efficiency for robustness, this method actively compensates analog non-idealities with adaptive error correction integrated tightly with ultra-low-voltage AIMC hardware, enabling scalable neuromorphic computing at power and area budgets unreachable by existing ferroelectric or 2D material synapse devices or fixed peripheral circuit approaches.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Develop an adaptive event-driven sparse coding neuromorphic framework that dynamically modulates sparsity and synaptic precision based on real-time physiological signal variability to achieve ultra-low latency and sub-μW power consumption in wearable health monitors.

BACKGROUND: Wearable health monitors require continuous, real-time processing of highly irregular and noisy physiological signals with minimal power consumption to maintain battery longevity. Current adaptive sparse coding methods lack fine-grained event-driven control and hardware-aware synaptic precision scaling, leading to excessive computational overhead, power dissipation, and latency that hinder long-term deployment. The open problem is enabling dynamic, data-dependent sparsity and synaptic precision adjustment directly within neuromorphic architectures to minimize energy use without sacrificing real-time responsiveness or accuracy.

APPROACH: We propose an event-driven sparse coding algorithm integrated with a hardware-mimetic neuromorphic architecture that employs two key innovations: (1) a dynamic sparsity controller that adjusts neuronal firing thresholds and pruning rates in response to instantaneous signal statistics (e.g., variance, entropy) of bio-signals; and (2) a multi-level synaptic precision scaling mechanism inspired by 2D vdW heterostructure opto-electronic synapses, allowing adaptive bit-width adjustment of synaptic weights to reduce power during low-activity periods. This algorithm tightly couples with a custom low-power neuromorphic processor implementation supporting asynchronous event-driven computation and synapse precision control. The system continuously monitors signal irregularity metrics to regulate sparsity and precision in situ, minimizing redundant computation and memory access.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on three benchmark biosignal datasets representative of wearable monitoring: (1) PhysioNet MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database, (2) WESAD (Wearable Stress and Affect Detection), and (3) PPG-DaLiA (Photoplethysmogram Dataset for Daily Life Activities). Baselines include: (a) Event-Driven Sparse Coding (EDSC) from the tournament winner, (b) Opto-electronic synapse model from the 2025 ReS2/h-BN/Graphene heterostructure study, and (c) Hardware-Implemented DropConnect ferroelectric synapse system (2025). Metrics: achieve ≥30% reduction in power consumption vs. EDSC baseline, ≤5 ms signal processing latency (30% lower than baselines), and ≥3% absolute F1-score improvement on arrhythmia classification tasks. Perform five independent training/evaluation runs to report mean ± std, and conduct ablation studies isolating sparsity control and synapse precision scaling contributions.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that treats sparsity and synaptic precision as fixed or statically tuned parameters, this work introduces a closed-loop adaptive mechanism that dynamically regulates both parameters event-by-event based on physiological signal irregularity, directly addressing power and latency bottlenecks in wearable neuromorphic health monitors. This adaptive fine-grained control tightly couples algorithm and hardware, enabling power and latency reductions beyond what static sparse coding or fixed-precision neuromorphic synapses can achieve.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Develop a noise-resilient, event-driven sparse coding neuromorphic architecture integrating adaptive thresholding and hardware-aware optoelectronic synapses for robust, real-time sensor fusion in autonomous vehicles under dynamic environmental conditions.

BACKGROUND: Current neuromorphic systems for autonomous vehicle sensor fusion struggle to maintain robustness and safety guarantees when processing high-rate, heterogeneous event streams corrupted by sensor noise and environmental variability. Existing sparse coding approaches inadequately address resilience to such noise, leading to unreliable decision-making and limited throughput in safety-critical driving scenarios.

APPROACH: We propose a neuromorphic framework combining (1) an adaptive thresholding mechanism that dynamically adjusts neuron firing thresholds based on real-time noise estimation from sensor inputs, (2) robust event-driven sparse coding algorithms that prioritize salient features while filtering noisy events, and (3) integration of high-precision multibit optoelectronic synapses based on 2D vdW heterostructures to realize hardware-efficient, low-latency weight updates supporting in-situ learning. This architecture is implemented on a scalable spiking neural network platform optimized for asynchronous event processing and sensor fusion tasks, explicitly designed for noise resilience and safety compliance.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the DVS Gesture Dataset, MVSEC (Multi-Vehicle Stereo Event Camera) dataset for event-based sensor fusion, and the KITTI Vision Benchmark Suite for autonomous driving perception tasks. Baselines include Robust Event-Driven Sparse Coding (the tournament winner), ConvNets for Energy-Efficient Neuromorphic Computing (2016), and 2TFGM Artificial Synapse-based neuromorphic networks (2021). Metrics measured are classification/recognition accuracy (>3% absolute improvement over baseline in noisy conditions), throughput latency reduction (>25% latency reduction), and energy consumption per inference (<20% reduction). Experimental protocol involves training and testing on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier with neuromorphic co-processors, running 5 random seeds per setup, with ablations on threshold adaptation, synapse precision, and event filtering.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that either focuses on hardware synapse improvements or algorithmic sparse coding without dynamic noise adaptation, our approach integrates adaptive thresholding with high-precision optoelectronic synapses in a unified event-driven neuromorphic architecture explicitly targeting robustness to sensor noise and environmental variability in autonomous vehicle sensor fusion, which no existing method guarantees at scale while maintaining low latency and energy efficiency.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a noise-resilient, adaptive analog neuromorphic circuit with integrated on-device calibration and algorithmic compensation for ultra-low power Edge IoT sensors, enabling robust lifelong learning under variable environmental noise without external recalibration.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing analog neuromorphic circuits for battery-powered IoT sensors fail to maintain adaptive learning capabilities under highly variable, noisy real-world environments due to limited on-device calibration and sensitivity to environmental drift, leading to degraded inference accuracy and increased power consumption for recalibration. Current solutions either sacrifice learning adaptability for power efficiency or require frequent human intervention, which is impractical for autonomous long-term deployments. The open problem is to sustain robust online learning in noisy, unstructured edge environments with minimal power and area overhead.

APPROACH:  
We propose a co-designed analog neuromorphic circuit and adaptive noise-aware learning algorithm featuring: (1) an integrated ultra-low power on-chip noise characterization module that periodically estimates environmental noise statistics without halting normal operation; (2) a closed-loop calibration mechanism that dynamically adjusts synaptic weights and neuron thresholds in the analog domain using these noise estimates; (3) a novel noise-resilient spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) algorithm enhanced with uncertainty-aware weight updates to prevent overfitting to transient noise; and (4) implementation on a custom mixed-signal chip fabricated in 65nm CMOS targeting sub-100nW power consumption per neuron. This synergy enables continuous, robust, adaptive learning under realistic noise conditions without external recalibration.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three Edge IoT relevant datasets: (1) DCASE 2023 Acoustic Scene Classification dataset for real-world audio event detection, (2) Intel Lab Environmental Sensor Dataset capturing temperature/humidity sensor drift and noise over time, and (3) OpenEDS 2020 dataset for eye-tracking under diverse illumination. Baselines include: (1) Analog neuromorphic circuits with conventional STDP from Indiveri et al. (2016), (2) Hardware-implemented DropConnect synaptic transistor system (Kim et al., 2025), and (3) Low-power dual-gate neurotransistor platform (Kim et al., 2023). Metrics: classification accuracy improvement >5% absolute over baselines under noisy conditions, power consumption <100nW per neuron (targeting 20% reduction vs dual-gate neurotransistor baseline), and system lifetime extension by >2x due to reduced recalibration needs. Protocol involves training/testing across 5 noise-level seeds, with ablation studies isolating algorithmic vs calibration contributions, all on a 16-core neuromorphic test chip prototype with 10^9 total synapses simulated.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works focusing solely on device-level energy reduction or fixed learning rules, our integrated system uniquely combines on-chip real-time noise characterization, adaptive analog calibration, and an uncertainty-aware STDP algorithm to sustain lifelong learning in highly variable noisy edge environments without external intervention or power-hungry digital overhead, a capability unaddressed by existing neuromorphic IoT sensor solutions.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a tightly integrated algorithm-hardware co-design framework that enables ultra-low-latency, energy-efficient on-chip continual learning for autonomous mobile robots by combining bio-inspired sparse spiking learning rules with novel mixed-signal neuromorphic hardware employing optoelectronic synapses and adaptive power gating.

BACKGROUND:  
Autonomous mobile robots operating in dynamic, unpredictable environments require neuromorphic systems capable of fast adaptation to sparse, noisy sensory inputs while maintaining strict energy budgets. Current neuromorphic solutions either focus on algorithmic efficiency without hardware integration or rely on novel devices without addressing real-time learning latency and power constraints simultaneously. This results in excessive computational overhead and energy consumption that limit continuous, on-the-fly learning in embedded robotic platforms.

APPROACH:  
We propose a co-designed framework where a bio-inspired, spike-driven learning algorithm leveraging sparse local Hebbian and reward-modulated plasticity is tightly coupled with custom mixed-signal neuromorphic hardware composed of ReS2/h-BN/graphene optoelectronic synapses for multilevel, low-power synaptic weight updates. Adaptive power gating dynamically adjusts hardware resource allocation based on sensory input sparsity and learning demand, minimizing energy use during idle or steady-state phases. The system supports on-chip continual learning with sub-millisecond latency for sensory-motor adaptation, validated using closed-loop control tasks in autonomous navigation.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark datasets relevant for embodied robotics and neuromorphic learning: the N-MNIST spiking vision dataset, the DVS Gesture dataset, and the Robot@Home sensory dataset. Baselines include (1) the Low-Latency, Energy-Efficient Algorithm-Hardware Co-Design for Autonomous Mobile Robots framework (the tournament winner), (2) the High-Precision Multibit Opto-Electronic Synapses system (2025), and (3) the Hardware-Implemented DropConnect Ferroelectric Synaptic Transistor (2025). Metrics include latency per learning update (target <1 ms, improving ≥30% over baseline 1), energy per synaptic update (target <10 fJ, improving ≥40% over baseline 2), and continual learning accuracy improvement on N-MNIST and DVS Gesture (>5% absolute F1 increase over baseline 3). Conduct experiments on a mixed-signal neuromorphic test chip fabricated in 28 nm CMOS with integrated optoelectronic synapses, training with 3 random seeds, including ablation studies on power gating and learning rule components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that isolates hardware device innovation or algorithmic improvements, this approach tightly co-optimizes spike-driven learning algorithms with mixed-signal optoelectronic synapses and adaptive hardware power management to achieve orders-of-magnitude improvements in energy and latency for on-chip continual learning in autonomous robots. Prior methods lack such integrated dynamic energy adaptation aligned with sparse learning, limiting their efficacy for real-time robotic applications.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an interactive, interpretable human-AI collaborative molecular design platform integrated with real-time experimental data feedback and regulatory compliance modules to enable domain experts in clinical research settings to effectively guide and validate AI-generated drug candidates.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative AI models for drug discovery primarily focus on molecule generation and optimization but lack interactive frameworks that empower clinical researchers with transparent decision-making tools and seamless integration into regulated experimental workflows. This creates a critical barrier in translating AI-generated molecules into clinically actionable therapeutics, as domain experts cannot sufficiently validate, interpret, or adapt designs in real time within compliance constraints.

APPROACH:  
We propose a human-in-the-loop generative design system combining a multi-modal Transformer-based molecular generator fine-tuned on chemical and clinical datasets with an interactive dashboard providing interpretable model explanations (e.g., attention maps, feature attribution) and regulatory checklist automation (e.g., FDA submission guidelines). The platform integrates live experimental data streams (e.g., assay results, ADMET profiles) using APIs to update molecule scoring dynamically, allowing researchers to steer the generation process. Collaborative features include versioned molecule libraries with provenance tracking and real-time multi-user annotation to support multidisciplinary teams.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark datasets including ChEMBL (version 29), MoleculeNet (specifically the QM9 and Tox21 subsets), and the OpenFDA drug labeling dataset for compliance feature validation. Compare against baseline models MolGPT, ChemBERTa, and GraphAF on metrics such as valid molecule generation rate (>95%), drug-likeness (QED > 0.7), novelty (>30% new scaffolds), and human-in-the-loop validation efficiency (reduce expert review time by >25%). Conduct ablation studies on interpretability and compliance modules across 5 random seeds using NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a training budget of 10k GPU hours.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior generative AI efforts that focus solely on molecule generation accuracy or single-target optimization, this work uniquely incorporates interactive interpretability and regulatory compliance within a real-time experimental feedback loop, enabling effective clinical domain expert collaboration and bridging a critical translational gap in AI-driven drug discovery pipelines.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Domain-Knowledge-Guided Graph-Based Few-Shot Generative Modeling with Biophysical and Pharmacophore Constraints for Rare Disease Molecular Design.

BACKGROUND:  
Rare disease drug discovery faces a critical challenge due to extremely limited labeled molecular data, causing existing generative AI models—dependent on large datasets—to fail in producing valid and biologically relevant candidates. Current few-shot generative methods lack integration of explicit biophysical and pharmacophore constraints, resulting in poor generalization and low generation validity for rare disease targets.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel few-shot graph-based generative model that explicitly embeds domain-specific biophysical constraints (e.g., binding site residue interaction profiles, molecular docking energy landscapes) and pharmacophore features (hydrogen bond donors/acceptors, hydrophobic centers) into the prior distribution of a variational graph autoencoder architecture. By integrating expert-curated rare disease molecular data (Orphanet, RareBind) with additional structural information from the Protein Data Bank (PDB) for rare disease targets, the model learns a constrained latent space facilitating high-fidelity molecular generation with as few as 10–50 training examples. A pharmacophore-guided sampling mechanism further filters generated candidates in-training to ensure biochemical relevance.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on curated rare disease molecular datasets including Orphanet rare disease targets, RareBind ligand-protein complexes, and BindingDB subsets specific to rare diseases. Baselines include standard few-shot molecular generators: MolGPT, GCPN (Graph Convolutional Policy Network), and meta-learning-based MAML-GEN. Metrics are:  
- Molecular docking score improvements (>10% better average binding affinity vs baselines) on AutoDock Vina,  
- Generation validity and novelty above 85%,  
- Predicted bioactivity accuracy improvement of >5% against QSAR models fine-tuned on rare disease targets.  

Training will be conducted on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a total compute budget of ~5,000 GPU hours, running five seeds for statistical robustness. Ablations will test the effect of removing biophysical constraints and pharmacophore guidance.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which either relies on large datasets or uses implicit domain knowledge, this method explicitly encodes domain-specific biophysical and pharmacophore constraints into the generative prior, enabling accurate molecule generation in the extremely low-data regime characteristic of rare diseases.
