This approach addresses the fundamental limitation of existing few-shot models that fail to generate relevant candidates due to lack of domain-informed priors, thus meaningfully extending the state of the art in generative AI-driven rare disease drug discovery.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a reinforcement learning framework for personalized drug design that dynamically adapts multi-objective reward functions using patient-specific multi-omics and clinical data, enabling robust molecular generation under extreme data sparsity and safety constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing generative AI methods for drug discovery fail to effectively personalize drug molecules due to their inability to handle sparse, heterogeneous patient data and dynamically balance complex efficacy-toxicity trade-offs in real time. This limitation restricts their application in precision medicine where individual patient variability and shifting therapeutic priorities must be accounted for to ensure both efficacy and safety.

APPROACH:  
We propose a reinforcement learning agent that integrates patient-specific multi-omics profiles (e.g., genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics) and clinical variables to dynamically shape a multi-objective reward function encompassing efficacy, toxicity, pharmacokinetics, and off-target effects. The agent employs meta-learning to rapidly adapt reward weights in response to evolving patient data and uses constrained policy optimization to ensure safe exploration within chemically valid and clinically relevant molecular spaces. The molecular generator leverages a hybrid graph transformer architecture for fine-grained molecular design, combined with a probabilistic uncertainty estimator to mitigate risks from data sparsity.

EXPERIMENT:  
Train and evaluate on benchmark datasets including ChEMBL 29 for molecular structures and activities, the Therapeutic Target Database (TTD) for target-disease associations, and the NCI-60 human tumor cell line panel for efficacy and toxicity profiles. Baselines include MolDQN, REINVENT, and GraphAF. Key metrics are >5% absolute improvement in multi-objective optimization score (combining efficacy and toxicity), >10% increase in molecular novelty and diversity, and reduction of unsafe molecule generation by at least 30%. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of 5,000 GPU hours, across 5 random seeds, including ablation studies on reward adaptation and safe exploration components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior generative AI models that use fixed or manually tuned reward functions and cannot adapt to sparse, patient-specific data in real time, our method explicitly incorporates dynamic multi-objective reward shaping driven by heterogeneous patient profiles and enforces safety via constrained policy optimization, enabling truly personalized, safe drug design tailored to individual therapeutic contexts.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Develop a physics-informed generative model framework that integrates real-time noisy experimental feedback from spectroscopy and microfluidic assays via a robust uncertainty-aware update mechanism to iteratively refine molecular designs during active drug discovery campaigns.

BACKGROUND: Current generative AI models for drug discovery lack the ability to incorporate real-time, noisy, and partial experimental data streams into the molecular generation loop, causing a disconnect between in silico design and wet-lab validation. This limits the models’ capacity to adaptively optimize candidates based on physical measurements, which is critical for accelerating closed-loop drug discovery workflows.

APPROACH: We propose a generative model architecture that conditions molecular generation on physics-based constraints and continuously updates latent representations through Bayesian filtering techniques that explicitly model experimental noise and uncertainty from spectroscopy and microfluidic assay outputs. The model leverages an efficient incremental learning algorithm enabling sub-second latency updates on streaming data, thereby allowing real-time iterative refinement of molecular structures under noisy feedback. Additionally, a hybrid representation combining graph neural networks (GNNs) and variational autoencoders (VAEs) ensures chemically valid and physically plausible candidate molecules.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate on the ZINC15 dataset, the QM9 molecular quantum chemistry dataset, and the CHEMBL bioactivity dataset, benchmarking against baseline models including JT-VAE, MoFlow, and GraphVAE. Metrics include molecular validity (>98%), novelty (>30%), and improvement in docking score prediction error (≥15% reduction RMSE) on downstream targets, as well as real-time update latency (<500 ms per update). Ablation studies will analyze the impact of uncertainty modeling and physics-based constraints. Experiments will be run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of 5,000 GPU hours across 5 random seeds.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior generative models (e.g., GPT-2 based molecular generators or static VAEs) that do not support real-time noisy experimental feedback integration, our approach explicitly fuses live physical measurement data into the generation loop with robust uncertainty quantification and rapid model adaptation—enabling a closed-loop, physics-informed molecular design process that bridges computational predictions and actual laboratory conditions.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a highly compressed, structure-aware graph generative model for molecular design that runs efficiently on mobile and edge devices, enabling real-time, on-site drug candidate generation with chemically valid and structurally accurate outputs under strict compute and memory constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art generative AI models for molecular design require substantial computational resources and memory, making them impractical for deployment outside centralized lab environments. This severely limits the accessibility of AI-driven drug discovery in resource-constrained, decentralized settings such as field clinics or mobile research units. The specific open problem is to maintain chemical validity and structural fidelity of generated molecules while aggressively compressing and accelerating graph generative models to run on mobile/edge hardware with limited resources.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel structure-aware graph generative architecture that leverages lightweight graph neural networks combined with knowledge distillation and quantization-aware training to drastically reduce model size and inference latency. The model incorporates chemically informed constraints directly into the graph generation process to ensure validity, uses a hierarchical graph representation to optimize memory usage, and employs edge-device-tailored inference optimizations (e.g., pruning, low-bit quantization). The pipeline is designed for on-device training fine-tuning using few-shot updates with local molecular libraries to adapt to specific therapeutic targets in resource-limited settings.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark molecular graph generation datasets QM9, ZINC250K, and MOSES for validity, uniqueness, novelty, and drug-likeness metrics. Compare against baselines: GraphVAE, JT-VAE, and MolGAN. Measure inference latency and peak memory usage on representative mobile platforms (e.g., ARM Cortex-A78, Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 1). Target improvements include >98% chemical validity with <100MB model size, inference latency under 200ms per molecule, and at least 30% reduction in latency and memory over MolGAN. Conduct ablation studies on model compression techniques and chemical constraints.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either focuses on high-accuracy but resource-intensive models or generative AI frameworks without deployment feasibility on edge devices, our approach simultaneously achieves state-of-the-art chemical validity and structural accuracy under aggressive compression specifically tailored for mobile/edge inference. This enables truly decentralized, real-time drug candidate generation inaccessible to previous generative AI drug discovery systems.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hierarchical, blockchain-sharded federated learning framework with adaptive client grouping and personalized aggregation to efficiently handle heterogeneous, large-scale non-IID data across diverse edge devices, significantly reducing ledger overhead and communication latency while improving model accuracy.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data faces critical challenges including client drift, scalability bottlenecks, and communication overhead, especially in large distributed cloud and edge environments. Existing blockchain-based ledger sharding methods (e.g., the winning work on scalable ledger sharding) reduce ledger size but do not explicitly address adaptive client grouping or personalized model aggregation strategies tuned for heterogeneity and statistical divergence. Moreover, prior methods either overlook dynamic client heterogeneity or induce high communication costs, limiting real-world applicability on large-scale, resource-constrained, and non-IID federated networks.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical federated learning architecture where clients are dynamically clustered based on data distribution similarity and device capability, with each cluster maintaining a sharded blockchain ledger to store update proofs and aggregation metadata. Within clusters, personalized aggregation is performed via a novel multi-level weighted consensus mechanism that blends local, cluster-level, and global model knowledge to mitigate client drift. The sharded blockchain employs parallel consensus protocols optimized for asynchronous updates and lightweight cryptographic proofs, minimizing verification overhead. Adaptive client grouping uses periodically updated embedding representations of client data distributions and system metrics to refine clusters, reducing inter-cluster statistical heterogeneity and improving model convergence.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on LEAF benchmark datasets (FEMNIST, Shakespeare), CIFAR-100 with artificially induced non-IID splits, and Google Speech Commands dataset to test multimodal non-IID scenarios. Baselines include Scalable Blockchain Ledger Sharding (the tournament winner), FedGKD (Local-Global Knowledge Distillation), HeteroSFL, and Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation. Metrics will measure ledger size growth (target ≥50% reduction vs baseline sharding), consensus latency (≤5% increase), model accuracy (≥3% absolute top-1 accuracy gain on CIFAR-100 non-IID), and communication cost (≥25% reduction). Experiments will run on simulated edge-cloud clusters with mixed hardware profiles (CPU-only IoT devices, GPU-enabled edge nodes), fixed 200 training rounds, and 5 random seeds. Ablations will test impact of adaptive grouping and multi-level aggregation separately.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior blockchain-based federated learning systems, this method integrates adaptive client clustering with personalized aggregation within sharded blockchain ledgers, enabling scalable, secure, and communication-efficient learning across highly heterogeneous and non-IID clients. Prior works either focus on ledger efficiency or client heterogeneity independently; this approach jointly addresses both at scale with falsifiable improvements in ledger overhead, latency, and accuracy on real datasets.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a dynamically adaptive hierarchical federated learning framework that integrates graph-based client clustering with personalized model aggregation optimized for heterogeneous IoT sensor networks experiencing both non-IID data distributions and frequent topology changes.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing federated learning methods for IoT sensor networks struggle with client heterogeneity, non-IID local data, and the dynamic, unreliable nature of IoT communication topologies, leading to degraded model accuracy and inefficient training convergence. Prior graph-based collaboration approaches fail to effectively adapt graph construction and communication strategies in real-time to node failures and asynchronous updates, and existing heterogeneous federated learning methods do not fully leverage network topology dynamics or personalized client models. This results in poor preservation of sensor-specific data nuances and limited robustness against network fluctuations in practical IoT environments.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical federated learning system where clients are first clustered dynamically based on real-time network topology and data distribution similarity using an adaptive graph neural network (GNN) framework that updates edge weights and connectivity to reflect node availability and link reliability. Within each cluster, a personalized model aggregation strategy leveraging multi-task learning regularizes local models toward cluster-level consensus while preserving client-specific features. A communication-efficient update mechanism selectively synchronizes cluster representatives with the global server asynchronously, minimizing bandwidth and mitigating the impact of unreliable links. Novel adaptive protocols detect and exclude unstable nodes temporarily to maintain robust training dynamics without sacrificing data diversity.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on three IoT-relevant, heterogeneous and non-IID benchmarks: Intel Lab Sensor Data (environmental sensing), PIRL HAR Dataset (human activity recognition from wearable devices), and N-BaIoT (network traffic for IoT malware detection). Baselines include Adaptive Graph-Based Client Collaboration (the tournament winner), FedGKD (Local-Global Knowledge Distillation), and HeteroSFL (Split Federated Learning for heterogeneous clients). Metrics will assess accuracy (targeting >3% absolute improvement over baselines), communication cost (aiming for >25% reduction in transmitted bytes), and convergence speed (expecting >20% faster convergence in rounds). Experiments will run on edge-simulated clusters of Raspberry Pi devices with controlled link failures, using 5 random seeds for statistical significance and ablation studies on graph adaptation and personalized aggregation components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike current state-of-the-art methods, this work integrates dynamic graph adaptation with hierarchical personalized aggregation explicitly tailored for IoT’s volatile topologies and heterogeneous non-IID data, enabling robust, communication-efficient federated learning that preserves client-specific data nuances despite asynchronous updates and node failures—capabilities neither addressed simultaneously nor effectively by existing graph-based or heterogeneous FL methods.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a resource-aware, adaptive personalized aggregation framework that dynamically calibrates model updates based on client computation capacity, connectivity patterns, and data heterogeneity, enabling robust federated learning on mobile edge devices with non-IID data and intermittent availability.

BACKGROUND:  
Mobile edge devices in federated learning often exhibit highly heterogeneous hardware capabilities, intermittent network connectivity, and non-IID local data distributions, which severely degrade global model convergence and personalization quality. Current aggregation methods either assume static client availability or homogeneous resources, failing to adapt to dynamic resource constraints and client drift simultaneously. The open problem is designing an aggregation mechanism that efficiently incorporates personalized updates while minimizing communication overhead and handling client heterogeneity and intermittent connectivity without sacrificing privacy.

APPROACH:  
We propose a Personalized Adaptive Aggregation with Resource-Aware Scheduling (PAARAS) framework that (1) profiles client resource constraints and connectivity patterns in real-time to schedule client participation, (2) incorporates a lightweight dynamic weighting scheme for personalized model aggregation that balances global and local updates per client heterogeneity and availability, and (3) integrates a communication-efficient update compression technique tailored to mobile edge constraints. PAARAS uses a client-side resource monitor module and a server-side adaptive scheduler that jointly optimize update frequency and model personalization, ensuring robust training despite non-IID data and unreliable participation.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate PAARAS on federated versions of CIFAR-100, FEMNIST, and Sentiment140 datasets under simulated heterogeneous device profiles reflecting diverse CPU, memory, and intermittent connectivity patterns. Compare against baselines FedAvg, FedDyn, and HeteroSFL. Metrics include test accuracy (target >2% improvement over FedAvg on non-IID splits), communication cost reduction (target >30% less data transmitted), and convergence speed (target at least 20% fewer communication rounds to reach target accuracy). Experiments use a cluster of NVIDIA Jetson Nano and Raspberry Pi 4 devices, with 5 random seeds for statistical significance and ablation studies on scheduling and compression modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods such as HeteroSFL and FedDyn that either focus on communication reduction or client drift separately under mostly static assumptions, PAARAS explicitly models and adapts to dynamic client resource variability and intermittent connectivity with personalized adaptive aggregation, providing a unified solution that simultaneously addresses computational heterogeneity, network unreliability, and statistical non-IID challenges in mobile edge federated learning.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a resource-aware adaptive neural architecture search framework within federated learning that dynamically personalizes ultra-lightweight models for heterogeneous, non-IID physiological data on ultra-low-power wearable health devices, optimizing inference efficiency and accuracy under strict energy constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing federated learning (FL) methods for wearable health devices fail to simultaneously address the triad of challenges posed by heterogeneous client hardware capabilities, highly non-IID physiological data distributions across users, and ultra-low power constraints for real-time inference. Current SOTA approaches either optimize static architectures or focus on communication/computation trade-offs but do not dynamically tailor model architectures personalized to both data heterogeneity and device resource budgets, leading to suboptimal accuracy or excessive energy consumption.

APPROACH:  
We propose Adaptive Federated Architecture Search (AFAS), a two-level optimization framework that (1) integrates client-side lightweight neural architecture search constrained by device-specific energy and latency budgets, and (2) jointly optimizes personalized architectures and federated aggregation via a multi-objective loss balancing model accuracy, computational cost, and communication overhead. AFAS incorporates heterogeneity-aware proxy metrics and hardware-in-the-loop profiling to guide architecture search on-device, while leveraging meta-learning to transfer architectural priors across clients with similar physiological signal distributions, enabling personalized yet collaboratively improved models.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate AFAS on three benchmark physiological signal datasets with diverse modalities and non-IID characteristics: PAMAP2 Physical Activity Monitoring, MIMIC-III Waveform Database, and WESAD (Wearable Stress and Affect Detection). Baselines include FedAvg, FedGKD (Local-Global Knowledge Distillation), and HeteroSFL (Split Federated Learning). Metrics: achieve ≥3% absolute improvement in average F1-score for activity/stress classification tasks, reduce average inference latency by ≥40%, and cut per-device energy consumption by ≥35% compared to static architectures. Experiments run on embedded-class ARM Cortex-M processors simulated with energy models, with 5 random seeds and ablation studies on architecture search objectives and meta-learning components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work such as HeteroSFL and adaptive distillation methods that focus on communication or training dynamics under heterogeneity, AFAS explicitly incorporates ultra-low-power hardware constraints into on-device neural architecture search personalized for non-IID physiological data. This direct coupling of architecture adaptation, data heterogeneity, and energy-aware optimization for federated health monitoring devices is unexplored and essential for practical deployment beyond static or server-heavy FL schemes.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Sparse Personalized Client Adaptation with Dynamic Memory Budgets for Federated Learning on Heterogeneous and Non-IID Data.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning on heterogeneous clients with non-IID data faces the dual challenge of maintaining high personalization accuracy while respecting the strict and varying memory constraints of edge devices. Existing personalization methods generate dense client-specific models that exceed limited device memory, hindering practical deployment and update efficiency. No current approach simultaneously addresses memory-constrained personalization with adaptive sparsity tailored to client heterogeneity and non-IID data distributions.

APPROACH:  
We propose a memory-aware federated personalization framework that dynamically adjusts sparse adaptation of a shared global model per client based on device-specific memory budgets and data heterogeneity. This method combines iterative structured pruning with parameter-efficient fine-tuning (e.g., low-rank adaptation and sparse mask learning) to produce compact client models that selectively activate subnetworks most relevant to local data distributions. A novel memory-budget scheduler controls the sparsity level per client during training to guarantee at least 50% model size reduction without accuracy degradation. Federated aggregation integrates sparse updates using a mask-aware weighted averaging scheme to preserve personalized features while mitigating client drift on non-IID data.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on federated variants of CIFAR-100, Sentiment140 (Sent140), and FEMNIST datasets to cover image classification and NLP with heterogeneous data. Baselines include full personalization methods (FedRep), sparsity-based adaptation (SparseFed), and local-global knowledge distillation (FedGKD). Metrics will measure personalization accuracy (>1.5% absolute gain over SparseFed), peak client memory footprint reduction (≥50%), and communication overhead (reduce by ≥20%). Experiments will run on simulated heterogeneous devices with memory constraints (128MB to 512MB), using 5 random seeds and ablation on sparsity scheduling and mask aggregation.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work such as HeteroSFL or FedGKD that either ignore memory constraints or lack fine-grained sparsity control, our approach uniquely integrates dynamic, client-specific memory-aware sparse adaptation with mask-aware aggregation to achieve scalable personalization under strict memory budgets on heterogeneous devices with non-IID data — a capability absent from existing methods.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a scalable, distributed physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework with novel multi-level domain decomposition and adaptive physics constraint enforcement to accelerate and stabilize billion-parameter global climate emulators on HPC clusters.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for high-resolution global climate models fail to scale efficiently across HPC clusters due to instability in distributed training and inability to enforce physics constraints consistently at extreme spatial-temporal resolutions. This limits their application to accelerate climate model simulations that require billions of parameters and fine-grained global grids, thus restricting advances in climate prediction fidelity and turnaround time.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical domain decomposition strategy that partitions the global climate domain into overlapping subdomains distributed across HPC nodes, combined with adaptive physics-informed loss weighting that dynamically adjusts constraint enforcement based on local solution error and physical inconsistency. This approach integrates scalable communication-efficient gradient synchronization with mixed-precision training and checkpointing to ensure numerical stability and enable training of PINNs with billions of parameters on exascale HPC architectures.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the NASA CMIP6 High-Resolution Atmospheric Model Dataset, the ERA5 Reanalysis Dataset, and the NCAR CESM Large Ensemble Dataset, comparing against baseline models including standard PINNs, DeepMind’s ClimateBench emulators, and the Physics-Guided Neural Networks (PGNN) framework. Metrics include >5% reduction in root mean squared error (RMSE) for temperature and precipitation fields, >30% reduction in training wall-clock time on Summit or Polaris HPC systems, and improved physical consistency measured by a >10% decrease in violation of mass and energy conservation laws. Experiments will run with three seeds each, including ablation studies on loss weighting schemes and domain partition sizes.

NOVELTY: Unlike existing PINN approaches that lack stability and scalability at extreme resolutions, this method enables stable training of billion-parameter physics-informed emulators across distributed HPC resources through hierarchical domain decomposition and adaptive physics constraint enforcement—capabilities that prior regional or low-resolution emulators and static physics constraints cannot achieve.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a cost-aware neural architecture search framework with adaptive multi-objective optimization that dynamically allocates heterogeneous cloud compute resources to design deep learning climate emulators optimized simultaneously for long-term accuracy, uncertainty quantification, and computational cost.

BACKGROUND:  
Current NAS methods for deep learning climate emulators focus on reducing search cost or improving accuracy independently but fail to jointly optimize emulator fidelity, uncertainty awareness, and resource expenditure. Existing approaches lack dynamic resource scheduling that accounts for varying compute costs of candidate architectures and neglect integrated uncertainty quantification needed for reliable climate projections, especially over multi-decadal horizons. This gap leads to prohibitively expensive or insufficiently robust climate emulator designs.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-objective Cost-aware NAS (CoNAS) framework that integrates (1) a cost-driven parallel scheduler allocating heterogeneous cloud resources (e.g., GPUs, TPUs, CPUs) based on real-time cost-performance trade-offs and (2) a joint optimization of emulator accuracy, uncertainty estimation quality (via Bayesian or quantile regression losses), and search cost. The NAS controller incorporates explicit uncertainty metrics during architecture evaluation and uses reinforcement learning to adaptively prioritize architectures that maximize accuracy while minimizing both computational expense and predictive uncertainty. We leverage asynchronous parallel searches with dynamic resource reallocation based on ongoing performance-cost feedback.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate CoNAS on CMIP6 multi-decadal 50-year climate forecast datasets (historical and future SSP scenarios), the CORDEX regional climate model downscaling dataset, and the MOMO-Chem multi-model surface ozone dataset. Baselines include vanilla NAS without cost-awareness (e.g., DARTS), state-of-the-art U-Net uncertainty-aware emulators, and fixed-architecture deep learning climate emulators (e.g., DeepESD, ArrowNet81). Metrics: reduce NAS search cost by ≥40%, achieve ≤5% lower Mean Absolute Error (MAE) on temperature and precipitation forecasts, improve uncertainty quantification calibration metrics (e.g., Expected Calibration Error) by ≥10%, and maintain or reduce inference latency by ≥20%. Experimental protocol: total NAS compute budget capped at 500 GPU hours on NVIDIA A100s, 5 independent runs for statistical significance, and ablation studies isolating cost-awareness and uncertainty optimization components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior NAS methods for climate emulators that treat accuracy and cost separately and ignore uncertainty, CoNAS jointly optimizes accuracy, uncertainty quantification, and compute cost using a novel dynamic multi-objective scheduler leveraging heterogeneous cloud resources. This enables efficient discovery of robust, uncertainty-aware emulator architectures suitable for multi-decadal climate projections—a capability not realized by existing transferability-focused or fixed-architecture deep learning climate models.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a parallelized Neural Architecture Search (NAS) framework incorporating multi-fidelity surrogate modeling and uncertainty-aware adaptive resource allocation to accelerate and improve deep learning climate emulators at scale on cloud infrastructures.

BACKGROUND: Current NAS approaches for climate emulators, including the tournament-winning parallelized NAS with asynchronous search and parameter sharing, reduce search time but remain computationally expensive and lack dynamic allocation of computational resources based on architecture promise, limiting scalability on large climate datasets. Additionally, existing methods do not explicitly incorporate uncertainty quantification to guide search efficiency and emulator robustness, hindering deployment in climate projection tasks that require reliability and interpretability.

APPROACH: We propose a scalable NAS framework combining (1) parallel asynchronous search with parameter sharing, (2) multi-fidelity surrogate models that predict candidate architecture performance using partial training and low-resolution data, and (3) uncertainty-aware adaptive scheduling that allocates more compute to promising architectures with high epistemic uncertainty estimates. This framework dynamically balances exploration and exploitation during search, reducing overall search walltime and improving final emulator generalization. The method leverages distributed cloud batch processing to execute architecture evaluations in parallel across multiple concurrency levels and fidelity tiers.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the GlobalClimateEmulator dataset, CORDEX regional climate projection data, and the CMIP6 historical simulations dataset. Baselines include the tournament-winning parallelized NAS approach, random architecture search, and fixed-architecture deep downscaling models (e.g., DeepESD, ClimateGAN). Quantitative metrics: reduce NAS search walltime by >40% vs. the winning baseline, improve emulator accuracy by at least 2% RMSE on temperature and precipitation fields, and decrease compute costs (measured in GPU hours) by 30%. Experimental protocol involves training on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a 2-week compute budget, performing 5 independent search runs, and conducting ablation studies on surrogate fidelity levels and uncertainty scheduling.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior climate NAS methods, this approach integrates multi-fidelity surrogate modeling with uncertainty-aware resource allocation to enable more efficient and robust architecture search at scale, reducing search time beyond the tournament winner’s 5x speedup while enhancing emulator transferability and reliability on diverse climate datasets. Prior work does not exploit uncertainty quantification to guide NAS resource scheduling or incorporate multi-fidelity evaluation in distributed cloud environments.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a Bayesian deep learning emulator framework with scalable, streaming variational inference to enable real-time, uncertainty-aware forecasting of near-term extreme climate events under sparse and non-stationary data conditions.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for climate models lack efficient inference algorithms capable of rapid updates from streaming observational data, which is critical for operational extreme event prediction where timely uncertainty quantification drives decision-making. Existing emulators typically rely on batch retraining or static uncertainty models that do not adapt dynamically to limited and evolving data streams, limiting forecast responsiveness and reliability in near-term extreme event contexts.

APPROACH: We propose a Bayesian deep neural network emulator incorporating stochastic gradient Markov Chain Monte Carlo (SG-MCMC) and amortized variational inference techniques to enable continual assimilation of streaming climate observations and rapid update of posterior predictive distributions. The model architecture will combine spatiotemporal convolutional LSTM layers with a heteroscedastic output to capture both mean prediction and dynamic uncertainty. We will implement a data assimilation pipeline integrating real-time satellite and in-situ measurements to perform online adaptation of emulator parameters and uncertainty estimates without full retraining.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluation will be conducted on the ERA5 reanalysis dataset, the CMIP6 historical and scenario simulations, and the Global Historical Climatology Network (GHCN) daily dataset for surface variables and extreme event labels. Baselines include the Bayesian U-Net with quantile regression (Uncertainty Quantification for Surface Ozone Emulators, 2025), DeepESN (Echo State Networks for climate forecasting), and deterministic downscaling DL emulators from the 2023 RCM emulator studies. We will measure continuous ranked probability score (CRPS), Brier score for event classification, and update latency, targeting >15% reduction in CRPS and Brier score relative to baselines, and sub-minute forecast update times on A100 GPUs. Ablations will isolate effects of streaming inference, heteroscedastic modeling, and data assimilation frequency.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that either focuses on static uncertainty quantification or offline batch emulator training, our method uniquely enables rapid, adaptive Bayesian inference for climate emulation with streaming data assimilation, directly addressing the bottleneck of real-time uncertainty-aware updates in near-term extreme event forecasting. This capability has not been demonstrated before at scale for climate emulators with sparse, non-stationary observational inputs.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
A hardware-aware multi-objective neural architecture search framework that jointly optimizes accuracy, uncertainty quantification, and real-device latency, incorporating structured pruning and quantization-aware training during search to produce embedded deep learning climate emulators with sub-30 ms inference latency, robust uncertainty estimates, and <5% accuracy degradation compared to unconstrained models.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep learning emulators for regional climate models often neglect hardware-specific latency constraints and uncertainty quantification, limiting their practical deployment on embedded devices like Raspberry Pi for real-time climate monitoring. While latency-optimized NAS with pruning exists, these methods do not integrate uncertainty-aware modeling essential for reliable climate predictions, nor do they consider quantization effects during NAS, resulting in models that either lack deployment readiness or fail to provide trustworthy uncertainty estimates.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-objective NAS framework that integrates (1) real-device latency profiling on embedded ARM devices, (2) structured pruning and quantization-aware training during architecture search to ensure efficient, low-latency inference, and (3) uncertainty quantification through Bayesian neural network layers or deep ensembles embedded in the searched architectures. The search objective combines predictive accuracy, calibrated uncertainty metrics (e.g., negative log-likelihood), and end-to-end latency benchmarks measured on Raspberry Pi 4 devices. The final models are validated for both runtime and probabilistic forecast quality by jointly minimizing latency and uncertainty calibration error with less than 5% accuracy degradation compared to unconstrained baselines.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the CORDEX regional climate dataset, CMIP6 GCM-to-RCM downscaled datasets, and the Surface Ozone Emulation Data from MOMO-Chem for multi-constituent predictions. Baselines include the original Latency-Optimized NAS with Hardware-Aware Pruning, Bayesian U-Net uncertainty emulators (2025), and deep learning downscaling models from 2023. Metrics include inference latency (<30 ms target), RMSE and MAE for prediction accuracy, Expected Calibration Error (ECE) for uncertainty quality, and negative log-likelihood. Experiments run with a 72-hour NAS compute budget on embedded-device simulators and physical Raspberry Pi deployments, with at least 5 random seeds and ablation studies isolating pruning, quantization, and uncertainty components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that optimizes only accuracy and latency post-search or ignores uncertainty, this method jointly optimizes hardware-aware latency, predictive accuracy, and uncertainty calibration inside the NAS loop with integrated pruning and quantization, enabling real-time, reliable embedded climate emulators with provable performance guarantees.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a domain-shift-resilient uncertainty quantification framework for deep neural networks in multimodal healthcare by integrating conditional mutual information recalibration with modality-specific domain adversarial networks to maintain calibrated uncertainty under varying domain and modality distributions.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods degrade substantially under domain shifts, especially in multimodal healthcare settings where training and testing data come from different hospitals with diverse modality availability and patient populations. Current information-theoretic uncertainty metrics fail to adaptively recalibrate under these distributional changes, leading to unreliable uncertainty estimates that hurt clinical decision-making trustworthiness. There is a critical need for UQ techniques that explicitly model domain and modality shifts to maintain reliability in real-world multi-source clinical environments.

APPROACH:  
We propose a unified framework combining conditional mutual information (CMI) recalibration with modality-specific domain adversarial training. Specifically, modality-specific domain discriminators are trained to generate domain-invariant features for each healthcare data modality (e.g., imaging, EHR, genomics). We then compute mutual information between model predictions and latent features conditioned on domain-adversarial embeddings to recalibrate uncertainty scores dynamically. This recalibration corrects for distributional biases across domains and missing modality combinations. The framework also includes a gating mechanism that weighs uncertainty contributions from each modality based on their presence and domain reliability. The entire system is end-to-end trainable, optimizing classification objectives alongside domain adversarial and uncertainty calibration losses.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on three multimodal, cross-institutional healthcare datasets with known domain shifts and modality variability: (1) MIMIC-CXR (Chest X-rays + clinical notes), (2) eICU Collaborative Research Database (multimodal ICU data), and (3) TCGA (The Cancer Genome Atlas, multi-omics + imaging). Baselines include: (a) Domain-Shift-Resilient Information-Theoretic Uncertainty (the tournament winner), (b) Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC) model (2025), and (c) Monte Carlo Dropout ensembles. Metrics:  
- Expected Calibration Error (ECE) reduction >5% absolute over baselines on shifted domains  
- Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC) for domain shift detection with >3% absolute improvement  
- Clinical outcome prediction accuracy improvement >2% absolute in cross-hospital evaluation  
Experiments will be run on 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with training budgets capped at 72 hours per method, using 5 random seeds for statistical significance. Ablations will isolate effects of modality-specific discriminators and CMI recalibration.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike the tournament-winning method which applies mutual information recalibration globally, our approach integrates modality-specific domain adversarial networks that explicitly handle missing and variable modality presence, enabling more granular uncertainty recalibration. This method addresses multi-source modality heterogeneity under domain shifts, a capability not provided by prior mutual information or latent space UQ methods.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a multi-objective neural architecture search (NAS) framework that simultaneously optimizes deep neural network architectures for calibrated uncertainty estimation and hardware-aware latency constraints on edge devices used in autonomous vehicles, incorporating probabilistic skip connections and active subspace parameterization for enhanced uncertainty quantification under strict resource budgets.

BACKGROUND:  
Current NAS methods designed for edge devices in autonomous driving focus primarily on accuracy and latency but neglect explicit optimization for uncertainty calibration, leading to unreliable uncertainty estimates critical for safety. Existing uncertainty quantification techniques (e.g., Bayesian methods, ensembles) are computationally expensive or poorly calibrated under edge constraints. There is an open problem in systematically discovering architectures that provide reliable uncertainty quantification within the stringent compute, memory, and real-time latency limits of automotive-grade edge hardware.

APPROACH:  
We propose a NAS framework that jointly optimizes for (1) uncertainty calibration via Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), and (2) hardware-specific latency measured on representative edge devices (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier, Tesla FSD chip). The search space integrates probabilistic skip connections (PSC) enabling intermediate-layer probabilistic modeling for deterministic uncertainty, and employs active subspace dimensionality reduction in network parameters to prune redundant directions, improving uncertainty robustness. The multi-objective optimization uses a constrained evolutionary algorithm that balances calibration metrics and latency budgets to output architectures that maintain high predictive reliability and meet real-time demands.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the Berkeley DeepDrive (BDD100K) driving dataset, the Waymo Open Dataset, and the KITTI Vision Benchmark Suite for perception tasks including object detection and semantic segmentation. Baselines include ResNet-50 with Monte Carlo Dropout, EfficientNet-B0 ensemble methods, and the state-of-the-art NAS-calibrated models from “Neural Architecture Search for Calibrated Uncertainty Estimation on Edge Devices in Autonomous Vehicles.” Metrics measured are Expected Calibration Error (target < 3%), Negative Log-Likelihood (target > 5% improvement over baselines), and inference latency (target < 30 ms per frame on Jetson AGX Xavier). Experimental protocol includes training each candidate 3 times with different seeds, performing ablation on PSC and active subspace components, and validation on held-out data splits.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior NAS methods that do not explicitly optimize uncertainty calibration under hardware constraints, this work integrates probabilistic skip connections and active subspace parameterization directly into the NAS search space, enabling discovery of architectures that are simultaneously well-calibrated and edge-efficient. This approach surpasses existing methods which either focus on calibration without hardware awareness or hardware-aware NAS without uncertainty guarantees, thus addressing the critical safety gap in real-time autonomous vehicle perception systems.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Latency-Aware Hierarchical Bayesian Neural Network (LA-HBNN) framework that adaptively allocates Bayesian uncertainty quantification computations across model layers under strict real-time latency constraints on edge devices, enabling scalable, calibrated uncertainty estimates with sub-30 ms inference latency on resource-limited hardware.

BACKGROUND:  
Current Bayesian uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for deep neural networks (DNNs), including variational inference and Monte Carlo sampling, incur substantial computational overhead, rendering them impractical for real-time edge applications with fixed latency budgets. Existing work, such as Latency-Constrained Bayesian Approximation for Edge DNNs, optimizes lightweight Bayesian approximations but treats the network as a monolithic unit, lacking fine-grained control of computational allocation across layers. This limits the ability to balance the trade-off between uncertainty calibration quality and latency, especially as model architectures grow deeper and more complex.

APPROACH:  
We propose a Hierarchical Bayesian Neural Network model where the Bayesian approximation fidelity and sampling complexity dynamically vary across layers based on their contribution to predictive uncertainty, guided by an adaptive scheduler optimized via reinforcement learning to meet strict latency constraints. The model leverages layer-wise variational inference with scalable low-rank approximations on early layers and more expressive full covariance variational posteriors on deeper, uncertainty-critical layers. The scheduler uses latency feedback from edge hardware (e.g., ARM Cortex-A77 CPUs or mobile GPUs) to maintain inference within a sub-30 ms budget while maximizing expected calibration error (ECE) improvements.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on MobileNetV3 and EfficientNet-Lite architectures using CIFAR-10, Tiny ImageNet, and ImageNet-1K datasets to benchmark accuracy, expected calibration error (ECE), negative log-likelihood (NLL), and latency measured on Snapdragon 8cx and Raspberry Pi 4 platforms. Baselines will include the original Latency-Constrained Bayesian Approximation for Edge DNNs, MC Dropout, Deep Ensembles, and Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC). Each experiment will be repeated over 5 random seeds; ablation studies will assess the impact of hierarchical Bayesian fidelity allocation and scheduler policies on accuracy-latency-uncertainty trade-offs.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that applies uniform or layer-agnostic Bayesian approximations under latency constraints, LA-HBNN introduces a hierarchical, layer-adaptive Bayesian inference mechanism coupled with hardware-informed scheduling, enabling fine-grained control over uncertainty estimation quality per layer within fixed latency budgets. This approach surpasses static approximations like those in Latency-Constrained Bayesian Approximation for Edge DNNs and probabilistic skip connections, providing the first scalable framework for calibrated Bayesian UQ tailored for deep and complex networks on real-time edge devices.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a real-time deterministic uncertainty quantification framework that fuses multi-sensor manifold embeddings with Lipschitz-certified uncertainty bounds augmented by adaptive active subspace projection to robustly handle rapid sensor distribution shifts in autonomous driving.

BACKGROUND:  
Current uncertainty quantification methods in deep neural networks (DNNs) for autonomous driving fail to maintain reliability under sudden and rapid sensor distribution shifts caused by environmental changes such as weather, lighting, or sensor noise. Stochastic approaches like Bayesian methods and Monte Carlo Dropout introduce latency and unreliability during rapid inference, while existing deterministic methods lack scalability or adaptivity to real-time manifold changes in multi-sensor fusion systems. There is a critical need for an uncertainty quantification approach that provides deterministic, certified guarantees under real-time constraints while adapting to dynamic sensor manifolds.

APPROACH:  
We propose Real-time Adaptive Manifold Fusion with Lipschitz-Certified Uncertainty and Active Subspace Projection (RAMF-LUAS), which constructs multi-sensor data embeddings on geometric manifolds and dynamically projects these embeddings onto low-dimensional active subspaces identified via sensitivity analysis. This projection reduces parameter space complexity while preserving uncertainty-relevant features. A Lipschitz constant certification framework provides deterministic uncertainty bounds that guarantee robustness under input perturbations and sensor distribution shifts. The system integrates an adaptive update mechanism that recalibrates manifold embeddings and active subspaces on-the-fly to respond to abrupt environmental changes, ensuring inference latency under 50ms.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate RAMF-LUAS on the nuScenes, KITTI, and Waymo Open Dataset for autonomous driving perception tasks involving multi-sensor fusion (camera, lidar, radar). Baselines include Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC, 2025), Monte Carlo Dropout-enabled ResNet-50, and Deep Ensembles on the same sensor fusion architectures. Quantitatively measure uncertainty reliability via Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), target >5% absolute reduction in ECE compared to PSC and >20% latency reduction relative to Monte Carlo Dropout, while maintaining mAP object detection accuracy within 1% of baselines. Run experiments on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a training budget of 100 GPU-hours, performing 5 random seeds per configuration and ablation studies isolating the impact of active subspace projection and Lipschitz certification individually.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike PSC or latent-space statistical inference methods that only fit probabilistic models post-hoc, RAMF-LUAS guarantees deterministic, Lipschitz-certified uncertainty bounds integrated directly into the fusion manifold representation with real-time adaptive subspace updates. This enables reliable uncertainty quantification under rapid distribution shifts without costly stochastic sampling, achieving lower latency and higher robustness than prior ensemble or Bayesian methods.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a unified framework that integrates probabilistic symbolic constraint checking with latent-space uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks to enable real-time, interpretable, and certifiable safety verification under tight latency constraints in autonomous driving.

BACKGROUND:  
Current autonomous driving systems inadequately combine deep neural network uncertainty quantification with symbolic logical constraint checking, leading to either unsafe decisions or overly conservative behavior due to separate handling of uncertainty and logic. Existing methods fail to provide interpretable, certifiable constraint compliance with strict inference latency guarantees (e.g., under 20ms), especially when the neural uncertainty is aggregated at the output level without leveraging richer latent-space representations or active subspace dimensionality reduction. This gap limits real-time safety-critical decision-making under uncertainty.

APPROACH:  
We propose a pipeline that first extracts a low-dimensional active subspace from the neural network’s latent feature space to efficiently model predictive uncertainty via a probabilistic skip connection (PSC) combined with Bhattacharyya coefficient-based distribution overlap metrics for fine-grained uncertainty estimation. This uncertainty is then tightly coupled with a symbolic constraint checker enhanced by probabilistic logical inference, enabling real-time symbolic constraint verification that explicitly propagates these uncertainty estimates to produce certifiable confidence bounds on constraint satisfaction. The system is optimized end-to-end to ensure inference latency under 20ms on embedded-grade GPUs, targeting autonomous driving scenarios simulated in CARLA.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on the CARLA autonomous driving simulator, the NuScenes dataset, and the Waymo Open Dataset, measuring constraint violation rate, inference latency, and uncertainty calibration metrics such as Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL). Baselines include MC Dropout, Deep Ensembles, Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC), and the Real-Time Symbolic Constraint Checking system from the tournament winner. Experiments will run on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier hardware with a compute budget of 48 GPU hours per run, using 5 random seeds and comprehensive ablations on the active subspace dimensionality and uncertainty propagation methods.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that treats uncertainty quantification and symbolic constraint checking as separate or loosely coupled modules, our method tightly integrates latent-space uncertainty models with probabilistic symbolic inference to yield interpretable, certifiable, and latency-bounded constraint verification. This framework leverages active subspaces for scalable uncertainty modeling and the Bhattacharyya coefficient for inter-class uncertainty, enabling real-time constraint compliance without the conservative over-approximation or latency violations that existing approaches exhibit.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Develop a lightweight, self-supervised generative foundation model architecture that enables rare cell type discovery directly from noisy, limited single-cell RNA-seq datasets in clinical environments with constrained computational resources.

BACKGROUND: Current single-cell foundation models rely on massive labeled datasets and extensive computational resources, which are unavailable in real-world clinical settings focused on rare or novel cell type identification. This creates a critical gap where rare cell populations—often key to diagnosis or treatment—remain undiscovered due to data scarcity and noise, as well as a lack of robust unsupervised modeling methods that can disentangle biological variability from technical noise on limited data.

APPROACH: We propose a resource-efficient variational autoencoder-based generative model with a biologically informed latent space regularization that disentangles technical noise from true biological signal without supervision. The model incorporates domain-specific inductive biases drawn from gene expression regulatory networks and leverages a contrastive learning objective to enhance representation robustness in scarce data regimes. Additionally, a novel curriculum learning scheme gradually introduces complexity to stabilize training on small, noisy clinical single-cell datasets.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on three benchmark datasets with rare cell populations: the Human Pancreas dataset (Baron et al.), the PBMC 68k dataset, and the Tabula Sapiens v2 dataset. Baselines include scVI, scGen, and SAUCIE. Metrics: rare cell cluster F1-score improvement by >5% absolute over baselines, adjusted Rand index (ARI) improvements >0.07, and training compute reduction by ≥50% compared to scVI. Experimental setup uses a single NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPU with 3 training seeds per dataset and ablation studies on latent space constraints and curriculum learning components.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior single-cell foundation models that require large labeled datasets and heavy compute (e.g., Nephrobase Cell+), this approach enables unsupervised rare cell type discovery directly on small, noisy clinical datasets while drastically reducing computational demands, a capability unattained by existing methods that fail to disentangle biological variability from noise under such constraints.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a lightweight, real-time multimodal foundation model architecture that integrates single-cell transcriptomics and spatial imaging data via contrastive embedding alignment optimized for clinical latency and resource constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current single-cell foundation models and multimodal biomedical AI systems deliver high accuracy but are computationally intensive and slow, making them unsuitable for real-time clinical diagnostic workflows where rapid inference under limited hardware is critical. Specifically, no existing model simultaneously achieves multimodal integration of genomics and imaging data at bedside speed while preserving diagnostic accuracy on clinically relevant tasks such as disease subtype classification or biomarker detection in single-cell spatial contexts.

APPROACH:  
We propose a two-branch lightweight encoder framework—one branch for single-cell RNA-seq data and one for spatial histopathology images—trained with a novel multimodal contrastive loss that aligns embeddings under strict memory and latency constraints. The RNA branch uses a parameter-efficient transformer variant with learned sparse attention, while the image branch employs a compact vision transformer distilled for fast inference. An embedding alignment module incorporates dynamic temperature scaling and feature pruning to optimize contrastive learning under runtime budgets. The model is further accelerated by on-device quantization-aware training and early-exit strategies during inference, ensuring sub-second latency on edge clinical hardware.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the Human Cell Atlas (HCA) single-cell RNA-seq dataset paired with the 10x Genomics Visium spatial transcriptomics dataset and the Kidney Precision Medicine Project (KPMP) multimodal kidney atlas. Compare against baselines including Nephrobase Cell+ (2025), Multimodal Contrastive Learning for Clinical Diagnostics (the tournament winner), and scBERT (2025). Metrics include classification accuracy on disease subtype tasks (>90% target), embedding alignment quality via mean average precision with >5% absolute improvement over baselines, and latency reduction aiming for <500ms per sample inference compared to >2s baseline latency. Experiments run on Nvidia Jetson AGX Xavier hardware with a max 50W power budget, using 5 seeds for robustness and ablation studies on embedding size, quantization level, and early-exit threshold.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike Nephrobase Cell+ and existing multimodal contrastive models which prioritize representation fidelity at the cost of heavy compute, our method explicitly incorporates latency and resource constraints into model architecture and training, enabling real-time clinical use. Prior single-cell foundation models do not simultaneously integrate spatial imaging data nor optimize for hardware-constrained, bedside diagnostic speed, a gap this work directly closes.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a generative pretraining foundation model for rare disease variant interpretation that integrates explicit evolutionary conservation priors with biologically informed loss functions to substantially improve sensitivity and interpretability for clinically critical rare variants in sparse, imbalanced genomic datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
Current variant interpretation models predominantly overfit to common variants and lack sensitivity to biologically significant rare variants, limiting clinical utility in rare disease diagnosis. Existing foundation models fail to incorporate domain-specific evolutionary and functional priors effectively, resulting in poor prioritization and interpretability on imbalanced datasets where rare pathogenic variants are underrepresented. This bottleneck critically hampers patient stratification and therapeutic decision-making.

APPROACH:  
We propose a generative pretraining framework that explicitly encodes evolutionary conservation signals (e.g., phyloP and phastCons scores) and integrates additional biological priors such as protein domain annotations and gene constraint metrics into the model architecture via specialized inductive biases. We introduce novel composite loss functions combining contrastive rare variant up-weighting with interpretability-driven regularizers to prevent overfitting common variants and enhance detection of pathogenicity signals from sparse data. Model training leverages self-supervised masked variant prediction augmented with these priors to learn robust, transferable representations for variant effect prediction.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on gnomAD v3.1 rare variant subset, ClinVar pathogenic vs benign variant classification, and the Rare Disease Variant Interpretation Benchmark (RD-VIB). Baselines include EVE, PrimateAI-3D, and DeepSequence. Metrics: achieve >5% absolute improvement in rare variant recall, >3% gain in precision-recall AUC on ClinVar pathogenic variants, and >10% reduction in false negative rate on RD-VIB rare variants. Protocol: train on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, 3 random seeds per model, ablations removing evolutionary priors and loss components to quantify contributions.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior single-cell or genomics foundation models that treat variant data agnostically, this method explicitly encodes biological priors into both model architecture and loss functions to directly address the rare variant imbalance and overfitting limitations noted in “Generative Pretraining for Rare Disease Variant Interpretation with Biological Priors.” This enables superior sensitivity and interpretability on clinically relevant rare variants beyond the reach of existing pretraining models that lack such domain-specific inductive bias.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable multi-omics graph foundation model that integrates heterogeneous molecular and spatial graphs with robust handling of missing and conflicting edges via hierarchical graph partitioning and uncertainty-aware edge imputation, enabling construction of comprehensive, biologically faithful tissue atlases at single-cell resolution.

BACKGROUND:  
Current foundation models for single-cell and multi-omics integration struggle with extremely large, heterogeneous tissue graphs that combine spatial cell-cell communication and diverse molecular interactions. These models cannot effectively manage missing or contradictory edge information at scale, leading to biologically incomplete or inaccurate atlases. The key open problem is creating scalable, robust algorithms that preserve multi-omics and spatial relationships despite noisy, incomplete graph data to produce high-resolution tissue references.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical graph foundation model that first partitions large heterogeneous multi-omics and spatial cell graphs into biologically informed modules using scalable graph clustering, then performs uncertainty-aware edge imputation based on multi-view contrastive learning to reconcile missing/conflicting edges. The model employs multi-omics embedding fusion via graph neural networks augmented with spatial attention, enabling joint representation learning across modalities and spatial context. A multi-scale hierarchical pooling preserves global tissue architecture while refining local cellular interactions. Training proceeds with masked edge prediction and multi-modal reconstruction losses to enhance biological fidelity.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the Human Cell Atlas (HCA) multi-omics spatial single-cell datasets, the Mouse Brain Atlas (Allen Brain Atlas single-cell multi-omics), and the Kidney Precision Medicine Project (KPMP) datasets. Baselines include Seurat v4 (weighted nearest neighbor integration), CellPhoneDB (cell-cell communication inference), and Glimma (graph-based multi-omics integration). Metrics: >3% absolute improvement in normalized mutual information (NMI) for graph clustering, >5% improvement in edge prediction F1-score, and >2% increase in biological pathway preservation score; ablation studies on hierarchical partitioning and edge imputation components. Protocol uses 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs, training for 72 hours with 3 random seeds to ensure reproducibility.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior single-cell foundation models and multi-omics integration tools that cannot scale or robustly handle incomplete/conflicting spatial and molecular edges, this method explicitly incorporates scalable hierarchical graph partitioning and uncertainty-aware edge imputation in a unified foundation model. This enables construction of biologically faithful, high-resolution tissue atlases from noisy, heterogeneous graphs at previously unattainable scales.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop Density- and Modality-Adaptive Graph Neural Networks (DM-GNN) that integrate multi-omics single-cell and spatial transcriptomics data with dynamic normalization layers to robustly model heterogeneous tissue microenvironments across diverse modalities and biological contexts.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing graph neural networks for spatial single-cell data, such as the Density-Adaptive Graph Neural Networks, address the challenge of varying cell densities but are limited to unimodal spatial transcriptomics and do not generalize across data modalities or multi-omics integration. The open problem is to build foundation models that maintain robust microenvironment representations across heterogeneous tissue architectures and modalities—such as spatial transcriptomics, proteomics, and epigenomics—enabling comprehensive, scalable modeling of complex biological systems that current approaches cannot handle.

APPROACH:  
We propose DM-GNN, which extends density-adaptive normalization by introducing modality-adaptive layers that adjust aggregation weights not only based on local cell density but also on modality-specific noise profiles and data sparsity characteristics. DM-GNN integrates spatial transcriptomics, spatial proteomics, and single-cell ATAC-seq data in a unified, multi-modal graph framework. It employs dynamic rescaling of neighborhood aggregation weights conditioned on local graph density and modality confidence scores, combined with cross-modality contrastive learning to preserve biologically meaningful rare cell niches and inter-cellular signaling across tissue types and species.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate DM-GNN on three benchmark datasets: 1) 10x Genomics Visium spatial transcriptomics data of human breast cancer, 2) CODEX multiplexed imaging proteomics data of mouse spleen, and 3) SNARE-seq single-cell RNA + ATAC multi-omics dataset from mouse brain. Baselines include Density-Adaptive GNN (original), SpaGCN, and Graph Attention Networks (GAT). Metrics: cluster separation via Adjusted Rand Index (target >5% absolute improvement), spatial pattern recovery measured by spatial autocorrelation (Moran’s I, target >0.1 improvement), and downstream cell-cell interaction inference accuracy assessed by area under ROC curve (target >3% gain). Experiments run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~10,000 GPU hours, performing 5 random seeds per setting and ablations removing either density or modality adaptation.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike Density-Adaptive GNNs and current single-cell foundation models that focus on single modality spatial transcriptomics, DM-GNN uniquely handles multi-modal graphs with dynamic normalization layers adaptive to both cell density and modality-specific data characteristics, enabling robust modeling of heterogeneous tissue microenvironments that vary in both cellular density and measurement modality—an advancement no prior method achieves.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an unsupervised continual learning framework for emergent communication in multi-agent systems that dynamically adapts symbolic languages in real time to rapidly shifting virtual environments using intrinsic self-supervised signals without explicit feedback.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication methods for multi-agent systems struggle with rapid environmental non-stationarity, requiring extensive labeled data or costly retraining to adapt communication protocols. This limitation prevents practical deployment in fast-changing domains like online gaming or robotic swarms where environment rules or agent behaviors frequently change. The specific open problem is enabling multi-agent communication protocols to evolve seamlessly and continually online without supervision, maintaining coordination and task performance despite unpredictable environment dynamics.

APPROACH:  
We propose an unsupervised continual learning approach that leverages intrinsic agent signals—such as prediction confidence, communication entropy, and task-related agent state changes—as self-supervised objectives to guide real-time evolution of symbolic communication languages. Agents maintain and adapt a shared latent communication embedding space via continual contrastive and predictive coding losses, enabling rapid protocol shifts without external labels or retraining. Additionally, a dynamic vocabulary pruning mechanism removes obsolete symbols while encouraging novel symbol invention, ensuring robustness and efficiency under non-stationary conditions.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on multi-agent reinforcement learning datasets with dynamic rules: the Google Research Football Environment with continuously changing game rules, the StarCraft II Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC) with evolving unit capabilities, and the PettingZoo MAgent environment with variable interaction rules. Baselines include Supervised Emergent Communication (Sigaard et al., 2023), ELICIT (Lazaridou et al., 2020), and MADDPG with communication (Lowe et al., 2017). Metrics: achieve >3% absolute improvement in task completion rate post-environment shift, reduce adaptation latency by 40% measured in episodes to stable coordination, and maintain communication entropy below 0.5 bits per symbol to ensure efficiency. Experiments run on NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPUs with five random seeds; ablation studies will isolate the impact of intrinsic losses and vocabulary pruning.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which requires external supervision or retraining cycles to adapt communication protocols (e.g., Sigaard et al., 2023), our method enables fully unsupervised, continual adaptation using only intrinsic signals, allowing rapid and robust real-time language evolution. Prior inductive biases or neuro-spike compression methods do not address protocol shifts under dynamic rules nor exploit continual self-supervision for online communication adaptation.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Develop an adaptive energy-aware emergent communication protocol for battery-powered autonomous drone swarms that dynamically balances signaling complexity and frequency based on real-time energy constraints and environmental conditions to maximize mission duration and coordination reliability.

BACKGROUND: Autonomous drone swarms operating in harsh, connectivity-limited environments face a critical bottleneck in communication energy consumption, which directly limits their operational endurance and coordination effectiveness. Existing emergent communication frameworks either ignore energy constraints or apply static, heuristic signaling strategies that fail to adapt to fluctuating battery levels and dynamic environmental interference, leading to suboptimal flight time and mission success rates.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework where each drone learns a continuous policy to modulate signaling frequency and message complexity conditioned on its own battery state and local environmental observations, incorporating a novel energy-cost regularizer in the reward function. The communication protocol evolves as a hierarchical latent vocabulary that compresses messages when energy is scarce but expands expressiveness under more favorable conditions, implemented via a variational information bottleneck adapted for energy-aware emergent signaling. Coordination is further enhanced by integrating an energy-predictive model that anticipates future communication costs and guides adaptive signaling decisions.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the AirSim drone swarm navigation dataset extended with energy consumption profiles (from Microsoft AirSim simulation) and the Multi-UAV Coordination Dataset (MUCv2) augmented with noisy connectivity scenarios. Baselines include the Energy-Aware Communication Protocols for Battery-Powered Autonomous Drones (the tournament winner), Neuro-spike Communication (2025), and the Firefly-inspired Vocabulary Generator (2022). Metrics: achieve >5% absolute increase in average mission flight time, >10% reduction in communication energy per timestep, and >3% improvement in task completion rate over Energy-Aware Communication Protocols baseline. Training will be conducted on NVIDIA A100 GPUs for 500k steps per run, with 5 independent seeds; ablation studies will test the impact of the energy-cost regularizer and hierarchical vocabulary components.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior methods that apply static or single-level energy constraints, our approach dynamically adapts both signaling frequency and message complexity in real-time based on internal energy states and external conditions, enabled by a hierarchical latent vocabulary and energy-predictive modeling. This enables a flexible trade-off between communication expressiveness and energy efficiency unattainable by existing emergent communication frameworks or neuro-spike single-bit signaling methods.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an adaptive, multi-modal emergent communication protocol that jointly optimizes energy-aware signal encoding and dynamic channel selection based on real-time agent energy states, failure risk, and environmental noise, enabling fail-safe, long-duration coordination in multi-agent systems beyond static or single-mode encoding schemes.

BACKGROUND:  
Current energy-adaptive communication methods like “Energy-Adaptive Signal Encoding with Failure-Resilient Protocols” focus on evolving encoding schemes that reduce transmission power and bandwidth based on local energy and failure rates. However, these methods rely on static channel usage and single-mode signaling, which limits robustness under highly variable or adversarial noise and multi-failure scenarios common in large-scale swarms. The open problem is designing a communication framework that adaptively selects among multiple communication modalities and encoding schemes in real time to sustain coordination despite energy constraints, channel impairments, and cascading failures.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-modal emergent communication architecture that integrates (1) evolutionary algorithms to adapt signal encoding parameters per modality (e.g., RF, acoustic, optical) based on agent energy reserves and local failure probabilities; (2) a reinforcement-learned channel selection policy informed by real-time environmental noise and interference metrics; and (3) a distributed failure detection and recovery mechanism that dynamically reroutes communication paths or switches modalities to maintain network connectivity. The method continuously updates agent-specific communication strategies to balance energy consumption, maximize information throughput, and minimize failure cascades, trained and validated via co-evolutionary multi-agent reinforcement learning.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate using three benchmark datasets/environments: the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE) with communication noise injection, the RoboCup Rescue Simulation Dataset extended with communication failure profiles, and the Swarm Robotics Simulation Dataset with heterogeneous agent energy models. Baselines include “Energy-Adaptive Signal Encoding with Failure-Resilient Protocols” (EASE-FR), the neuro-spike communication framework from 2025, and the Firefly-inspired vocabulary generator (2022). Metrics will measure swarm task completion rate (target >5% absolute improvement), average agent lifetime increase (target >15%), communication throughput stability under noise (reduce packet loss by 20%), and failure recovery time (reduce by 30%). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a 500k-step training budget, averaging results over 10 random seeds. Ablations will isolate the impact of multi-modal encoding, channel selection policies, and failure recovery modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that employs fixed or single-mode communication encoding (EASE-FR) or minimalist spike-like signals without adaptive channel choice (neuro-spike comm), our method uniquely combines multi-modal adaptive encoding with real-time channel selection and distributed failure recovery, enabling robust, energy-efficient emergent communication that sustains swarm coordination under diverse, high-noise, and failure-prone conditions. This holistic integration has not been achieved in existing emergent communication systems.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hierarchical neuro-symbolic emergent communication framework that adaptively compresses and parses multi-agent messages under 5ms latency constraints to enable scalable, ultra-low latency coordination in autonomous vehicle platoons and heterogeneous multi-agent systems.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing neuro-symbolic communication protocols for autonomous vehicle platooning, such as "Low-Latency Neuro-symbolic Message Parsing for Autonomous Vehicle Platooning," achieve sub-10ms latency but struggle to scale efficiently as platoon size and message complexity grow, limiting real-world deployment. Current methods fail to dynamically balance the trade-off between message expressiveness and latency under strict real-time safety constraints, especially in heterogeneous multi-agent settings requiring flexible interoperability. There is a need for a communication protocol that significantly reduces latency below 10ms while maintaining symbolic reasoning capabilities and scalability in dynamic environments.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical, adaptive neuro-symbolic communication architecture combining (1) a multi-level neural encoder that learns compressed latent representations of symbolic messages optimized for latency and bandwidth, (2) an ultra-fast symbolic decoder that incrementally parses these compressed messages with early-exit mechanisms conditioned on task urgency, and (3) a dynamic message complexity controller that adjusts communication granularity based on platoon size, environmental context, and agent heterogeneity. This system integrates reinforcement learning-driven policy to optimize the trade-off between latency, message fidelity, and platoon stability.
